event_blocks.summary: A black screen displays a white text warning, 'This film contains images which some viewers may find distressing,' read aloud by a male narrator in a clear, somber tone, accompanied by a soft, melancholic string melody. The screen transitions to a dark background as Francisco Goya's 'Saturn Devouring His Son' painting emerges from the right, with the camera slowly zooming in on the grotesque depiction of a monstrous, emaciated figure with wild hair and wide eyes, violently consuming a small, pale, bloody human form. The narrator explains that in 1819, Goya, isolated after personal losses and in his seventies and deaf, withdrew from public life. Dim lighting accentuates the painting's nightmarish atmosphere, highlighting the blood-streaked mouth, hollow eyes, and contorted expression of madness. The narrator concludes that Goya, who once painted religious subjects, began creating 'terrifying demonic, raw and brutal works' without 'even a hint of God,' aligning with the primal intensity of the devouring figure's face in extreme close-up, all underscored by the continuous, somber music.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a monstrous, gaunt figure with wild hair and wide eyes devouring a small, bloodied human form; characterized by violent brushwork, stark contrasts, and a deeply unsettling atmosphere. The narrator contextualizes this work as one of Goya's later 'terrifying demonic, raw and brutal works,' created after he withdrew from public life in 1819, isolated, sick, and completely deaf, marking a profound departure from his earlier religious themes and lacking 'even a hint of God'.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with a rapid montage of famous artworks, including Hokusai’s *The Great Wave off Kanagawa*, Warhol’s *Marilyn Monroe*, da Vinci’s *Mona Lisa*, and Van Goya’s *The Two Fridas*, accompanied by melancholic Spanish acoustic guitar music, before focusing on Van Gogh’s *The Starry Night* with the title 'GREAT ART EXPLAINED'. A male narrator then introduces Francisco Goya’s *The Black Paintings*, created between 1819 and 1823 in Spain, stating they convey his pessimistic view of humanity. The first painting shown is *Witches’ Sabbath* (1819–1823), a dark, chaotic scene with grotesque figures and a demonic central figure. The narrator explains Goya's shift from a celebrated painter to an isolated artist creating works on the walls of his secluded farmhouse, never intended for public viewing, as *Two Old Men Eating Soup* (1819–1823) is displayed, showing emaciated figures sharing a meager meal. A contrast is presented with the earlier, brightly lit, formal portrait *The Family of the Infante Don Luis* (1783). The dark tone resumes with *Women Laughing* (1819–1823), featuring two women with unsettling grins, followed by *A Pilgrimage to San Isidro* (1819–1823), a frenzied religious procession. The narrator notes that Goya never named these works, which were later called the Pinturas Negras due to their dark colors. The video concludes by showing visitors in a dimly lit museum gallery observing the Black Paintings under focused lighting, highlighting their somber, haunting presence.

Museum Gallery.description: A dimly lit, spacious art gallery with polished floors and white walls, featuring large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series displayed under focused lighting, with visitors observing them in quiet contemplation; these paintings were originally created by Goya in total isolation on his farmhouse walls, never intended for public viewing, and were only posthumously named 'Pinturas Negras' or 'Black Paintings'.

The Family of the Infante Don Luis Paint.description: A formal, brightly lit portrait by Francisco Goya of an aristocratic family in 18th-century attire, featuring rich colors, elegant poses, and a sense of refined social order.

Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting.description: A somber, dark painting by Francisco Goya showing two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair, rendered in rough, expressive strokes; created between 1819-1823 during Goya's later, pessimistic period, in total isolation on his farmhouse walls, and posthumously named as part of the 'Pinturas Negras' or 'Black Paintings' series.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene, characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere; created between 1819-1823 during Goya's later, pessimistic period, in total isolation on his farmhouse walls, and posthumously named as part of the 'Pinturas Negras' or 'Black Paintings' series.

A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting.description: A crowded, frenzied scene by Francisco Goya showing a religious procession with figures in ecstatic or frenzied states, their faces contorted, rendered in dark, expressive brushwork; created between 1819-1823 during Goya's later, pessimistic period, in total isolation on his farmhouse walls, and posthumously named as part of the 'Pinturas Negras' or 'Black Paintings' series.

Women Laughing Painting.description: A dark, unsettling painting by Francisco Goya depicting two women with wide, distorted grins, their features blurred and eerie, evoking a sense of madness or mockery; created between 1819-1823 during Goya's later, pessimistic period, in total isolation on his farmhouse walls, and posthumously named as part of the 'Pinturas Negras' or 'Black Paintings' series.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a rapid visual montage of iconic artworks, including Hokusai’s *The Great Wave off Kanagawa*, Warhol’s *Marilyn Monroe* silkscreen, da Vinci’s *Mona Lisa*, and Van Goya’s *The Two Fridas*, before transitioning to Van Gogh’s *The Starry Night* with the title 'GREAT ART EXPLAINED' overlaid. Throughout this sequence, a melancholic, Spanish-sounding acoustic guitar plays. The focus then shifts to Francisco Goya’s *The Black Paintings* as a male narrator begins to speak, explaining that "Francisco Goya's Black Paintings were created during his later years in Spain, from around 1819 to 1823, and convey his deeply pessimistic view of humanity." The first painting shown is *Witches’ Sabbath* (1819–1823), a dark, chaotic scene filled with grotesque figures and a central demonic figure, rendered in thick, expressive brushstrokes. The narrator continues, detailing how Goya "went from being the most famous painter in the country, idolized by an adoring public, to an artist who created his works in total isolation, on the bare walls of his secluded farmhouse, which were never intended for public viewing." This context accompanies the display of *Two Old Men Eating Soup* (1819–1823), depicting two emaciated, almost skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair. A visual contrast is then drawn with *The Family of the Infante Don Luis* (1783), a brightly lit, formal portrait of an aristocratic family in rich attire, suggesting Goya’s earlier, more polished style. The sequence returns to the dark tone with *Women Laughing* (1819–1823), showing two women with unsettling, wide grins, their features distorted and eerie. Next, *A Pilgrimage to San Isidro* (1819–1823) appears, a crowded, frenzied scene of people in a religious procession, their faces contorted in ecstasy or madness. The narrator adds that Goya "never named them or explained them, and only after his death would they be named the Pinturas Negras, the Black Paintings, because of the dark colors he used." The video then transitions to a real-world museum setting, where visitors walk through a dimly lit gallery, observing the Black Paintings displayed on the walls under focused lighting, emphasizing their somber, haunting presence, grounding the viewer in the contemporary experience of these historically significant works.

Melancholy classical guitar music introduces a blurred 'Self-Portrait at an Easel' (1790), hinting at Goya’s earlier, more vibrant works. An aerial view of a Madrid bullring, dated 1775, marks Goya's arrival in the city and his commencement of work on "tapestry cartoons for the royal residences," designs for tapestries in "royal palaces," which facilitated his "first contact with the Spanish Royal Family." This formative period, where he "learned to tell a story" in the tapestry factory, is illustrated by paintings like 'Majo With Guitar (Detail)' (1779) and 'Boys Playing at Being Soldiers' (1777). The narrative progresses to 'Charles IV of Spain and his Family (Detail)' (1800), featuring Goya’s self-portrait, as the narrator reveals Goya's 1789 appointment as "court painter" and his eventual service to "three Spanish kings." The sequence culminates with formal portraits, 'Charles III in Hunting Dress' (1786), identified as "Charles III, the great reformer," and 'Charles IV in Red' (1789), showcasing Goya’s artistic transition from lightheartedness to profound introspection.

Majo With Guitar (Detail) Painting.description: A close-up detail of a man in a tricorn hat with a contemplative, upward gaze, from Francisco Goya’s 1779 painting, showcasing his early, more naturalistic and expressive style.

Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting.description: A portrait of Francisco Goya (1790) in a red dress and black hat, holding a paintbrush and palette, standing before an easel, representing his earlier, more confident and vibrant artistic phase.

Charles III in Hunting Dress Painting.description: A portrait of Charles III (1786) in a gray hunting coat, white cravat, and tricorn hat, holding a rifle, set against a neutral background, reflecting the regal yet active persona of the king, whom the narrator identifies as "Charles III, the great reformer."

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of the 'Fernando VII Painting', identified as the tyrant king, followed by the 'Joseph Bonaparte Painting', depicted in opulent regalia as the occupying French king. The focus then shifts to Goya's artistic evolution, starting with a youthful 'Self-Portrait Painting' from 1771, showcasing his early career as a naturalistic painter of the upper classes. A sinister turn in Goya's health is illustrated by a later 'Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painting', where a frail Goya is supported by his doctor after a mysterious illness in 1792 rendered him completely deaf. This personal affliction coincided with Spain's enormous political and social upheavals, visually represented by the chaotic battle scene in 'The Second of May 1808 Painting', filled with soldiers and horses amidst intense battle sounds. During this tumultuous period, Goya experienced an unexpected revolution in his art, moving from light to dark, as evidenced by the intercutting of the delicate 'The Marquise de la Solana Painting' with his darker etchings: 'And They are Like Wild Beasts Etching', 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters Etching', and 'A Way of Flying Etching'. These works, depicting violent, monstrous, and surreal figures, underscore how his profound deafness impacted his art, leading him to explore a more personal vision.

Two Old Men Painting.description: A dark, somber painting of two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger, rendered in rough, expressive strokes, painted between 1819–1823.

Joseph Bonaparte Painting.description: A full-length portrait of Joseph Bonaparte in opulent white and gold royal attire, seated on a throne with a golden drape behind him, painted in 1808, identified by the narrator as the 'occupying French king'.

Self-Portrait Painting.description: A youthful self-portrait of Francisco Goya with dark, wavy hair and a somber expression, painted in 1771.

The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters Et.description: A black-and-white etching of a sleeping man surrounded by owls and bats, symbolizing the dangers of ignorance, painted in 1799.

Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painti.description: A somber self-portrait of Goya appearing frail and ill, supported by Doctor Arrieta who holds a glass, painted in 1820, reflecting Goya's health taking a 'sinister turn' after developing a 'mysterious illness' in 1792 that left him 'completely deaf', profoundly impacting his art.

The Second of May 1808 Painting.description: A chaotic battle scene depicting soldiers, civilians, and horses in violent struggle, with a central figure on a white horse raising a sword, painted in 1814, visually representing the 'enormous political and social upheavals' in Spain, accompanied by battle sounds, during which Goya was 'in the thick of it'.

The Marquise de la Solana Painting.description: A delicate portrait of a woman in a white shawl with a pink flower in her hair, painted in 1793.

A Way of Flying Etching.description: A grotesque etching of a winged figure with a long tongue flying over a landscape, published in 1864.

And They are Like Wild Beasts Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching depicting violent, animalistic figures in a frenzied struggle, published in 1863.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with Francisco Goya's dark etching 'A Way of Flying,' published in 1864, showcasing a grotesque winged figure, as a narrator characterizes Goya's work as increasingly visionary and enigmatic, and his most subversive. This transitions to a 1799 self-portrait of Goya, followed by his 'Los Caprichos' etchings, including a man attacked by an owl, with the narrator noting Goya created this series in his 50s. A close-up of 'A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men!' (Plate 39), published in 1863 and part of 'The Disasters of War' produced in his 60s, reveals a muscular, horned figure dragging a corpse. The scene then shifts to a 1795 painted self-portrait of Goya, as the narrator remarks on Goya's later work often being produced in secret, portraying him as a pragmatist in public but a revolutionary in private. The visual narrative moves to historical paintings, starting with Antoine-Jean Gros's 'Napoleon Accepts the Surrender of Madrid' (1810), depicting Napoleon in military regalia, while the narrator details Napoleon Bonaparte's 1808 invasion of Spain, his ousting of Ferdinand VII, and crowning his brother Joseph Bonaparte as King. This is followed by Jacques Louis-David's 'Coronation of Napoleon' (1804), showing Napoleon crowning himself, and an exterior shot of the grand Royal Palace of Madrid. A portrait of Nicolas Guye, a French Army officer, painted by Goya in 1810 during the French occupation, appears, with the narrator explaining Goya remained in Madrid and worked for the French, a decision with serious implications. The segment concludes with Carle Verner's 'Napoleon on the Outskirts of Madrid' (1812), depicting Napoleon in a tense meeting, and Paul Delaroche's formal portrait of Napoleon (1838), as the narrator reflects on the complicated nature of the French invasion for Spanish liberals like Goya, all set against a serious instrumental background score.

Coronation of Napoleon Painting.description: A grand historical painting by Jacques Louis-David (1804) showing Napoleon placing a crown on his own head, surrounded by courtiers and clergy, with a woman in white in the background, rendered in rich, detailed brushwork, depicting Napoleon Bonaparte's self-coronation.

Napoleon Accepts the Surrender of Madrid.description: A historical painting by Antoine-Jean Gros (1810) depicting Napoleon in a white uniform with a red sash, standing before Spanish officials who are surrendering, set in a tented camp with dramatic lighting and smoke, illustrating Napoleon Bonaparte's invasion of Spain in 1808.

Napoleon Painting.description: A formal portrait by Paul Delaroche (1838) of Napoleon in a dark military uniform with gold epaulettes and medals, seated against a deep red background, conveying authority and solemnity, representing Napoleon Bonaparte.

Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Capr.description: A profile self-portrait of Francisco Goya from 1799, wearing a tall black hat and formal coat, with the inscription 'Franco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor' beneath, rendered in fine etching lines, created when Goya was in his 50s as part of his 'Los Caprichos' series.

Self-Portrait Painting.description: A painted self-portrait of Francisco Goya from 1795, showing him with dark, curly hair, a serious expression, and a colorful striped cravat, holding a palette, set against a neutral background.

Napoleon on The Outskirts of Madrid Pain.description: A historical painting by Carle Verner (1812) depicting Napoleon in military attire, standing among Spanish figures in a tense negotiation, with smoke and chaos in the background, suggesting war, reflecting the period of Napoleon Bonaparte's invasion and French occupation of Spain.

A Way of Flying Etching.description: A grotesque black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, depicting a winged, demonic figure with a long tongue flying over a landscape, accompanied by smaller flying creatures, rendered in stark, expressive lines.

Nicolas Guye, French Army Painting.description: A formal portrait by Francisco Goya (1810) of Nicolas Guye in a French military uniform with gold epaulettes, red sash, and medals, painted during the French occupation of Spain, a period when Goya worked for the French.

The Royal Palace of Madrid.description: A wide exterior shot of the grand, neoclassical Royal Palace of Madrid, featuring numerous windows, columns, and a green dome, under an overcast sky.

event_blocks.summary: Napoleon, depicted in a formal military portrait, arrived with promises of restoring democracy, a stark contrast to the 'The Disappointed King of Spain,' where Charles IV's despair was mocked by a Napoleon-like figure, while Ferdinand was a despised dictator. Initially, Goya and his liberal friends saw French rule as a 'great hope of liberalization and enlightenment,' symbolized by 'Truth, Light, Philosophy and Reason,' a sentiment that shifted when Napoleon abolished the 'hated Inquisition.' However, Goya's perspective darkened with the 'violent subjugation of his countrymen,' as seen in 'And There’s Nothing to be Done,' and the subsequent 'war and its ensuing atrocities' deeply affected his work, exemplified by the violent 'Attack of a Woman.' The 'darker subject matter' and 'deep fear for Spain's future' became evident in etchings like 'Simpleton’s Folly.' Spanish rebels initiated 'guerrilla warfare,' a dynamic struggle depicted in 'Guerrilla Warfare,' leading to Napoleon's eventual expulsion in 1813, culminating in the symbolic restoration of King Ferdinand VII, who had been in exile, as shown in 'Landing of Ferdinand VII in El Puerto.'

Simpleton’s Folly.description: A grotesque etching by Francisco Goya (1799) of a man in a state of madness, clutching his head, representing the 'darker subject matter' and 'deep fear for Spain's future' seen in Goya's later work.

Landing of Ferdinand VII in El Puerto.description: A grand, celebratory painting by José Aparicio (1823) depicting King Ferdinand VII’s return, with nobles, soldiers, and civilians gathered on a ship under a bright sky, symbolizing restoration and hope, as the audio confirms 'King Ferdinand the Seventh, who had been living in exile'.

Attack of a Woman.description: A dark, violent oil painting by Francisco Goya (1811) showing a woman being assaulted by a monstrous figure, with another figure in the background holding a torch, illustrating how 'the war and its ensuing atrocities affected him and his work deeply'.

The Disappointed King of Spain.description: A satirical political cartoon by Isaac Cruikshank (1808) depicting King Charles IV in royal attire, seated on a broken chair with arms raised in despair, as his crown flies away labeled 'Sincerity,' while a figure resembling Napoleon mocks him with a speech bubble, appearing as the audio mentions 'Ferdinand was a despised dictator', setting a context of monarchy and political upheaval.

Condemned by The Inquisition.description: A dark, dramatic painting by Eugenio Lucas Velázquez (1860) depicting a nightmarish scene of torture and persecution, with figures in conical hats and chains, illuminated by flickering torchlight, which Napoleon is noted to have 'abolish[ed]'.

The Third of May 1808.description: A powerful oil painting by Francisco Goya (1814) showing a man with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies, under a dark sky, appearing as the audio discusses the 'repercussions' and Napoleon being 'forced out in 1813'.

Truth, Light, Philosophy and Reason.description: A classical allegorical engraving by Benoît-Luis Prévost (1764) portraying robed figures in a mythological setting, with one holding a torch and another a laurel wreath, symbolizing enlightenment ideals, visually representing the 'liberalization and enlightenment' that Goya and his friends initially hoped for from French rule.

Napoleon Painting.description: A formal portrait by Paul Delaroche (1838) of Napoleon in a dark military uniform with gold epaulettes and medals, seated against a deep red background, conveying authority and solemnity, introduced as having 'came in with talks of restoring democracy' and later noted for abolishing 'the hated Inquisition' and eventually being 'forced out in 1813'.

And There’s Nothing to be Done (Plate 15.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man standing over a fallen figure, evoking despair and helplessness, reflecting Goya's changed opinion of the French after 'personally witnessing the violent subjugation of his countrymen'.

Guerrilla Warfare.description: A colorful, dynamic painting by Roque Gameiro (1917) depicting Spanish guerrillas ambushing French troops in a rocky landscape, directly illustrating the 'campaign of guerrilla warfare' started by Spanish rebels.

This footage visually underscores the narrator's description of Ferdinand VII 'clamping down on dissent with an iron fist' and dragging Spain 'back into the Dark Ages'.

Dark Goya (1792 onwards) Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching depicting a violent, grotesque scene of figures in struggle, with a man being attacked by others in a dark, confined space, rendered in dense cross-hatching. The narrator links this to Goya's series on 'the monstrosities of war and human behavior'.

Charles III Painting.description: A portrait of Charles III as an adult, wearing armor with a red and gold sash, seated with a composed expression, set against a dark background with a draped golden curtain. The narrator identifies him as 'the beloved Charles III,' a king who believed in religious reform and a constitutional monarchy, aligning with Enlightenment principles.

Ferdinand VII Painting.description: A formal portrait of King Ferdinand VII in a red vest and black coat with a sash, seated beside a crown, rendered in rich, dark tones. The narrator explicitly names him as 'Ferdinand VII' and describes him as an 'absolute monarch' who 'rejected any type of modernization,' 'reinstated the Inquisition,' 'gave the church more power,' 'revoked the constitution,' 'clamped down on dissent with an iron fist,' and 'began the process of dragging Spain back into the Dark Ages'.

The Inquisition Tribunal Painting.description: A dark, crowded scene of a religious trial with figures in conical hats and robes, illuminated by a single light source, evoking tension and oppression. This painting visually represents the 'Inquisition' that the narrator states Ferdinand VII 'reinstated'.

A Philosopher Lecturing on the Orrery Pa.description: A dramatic scene with a central figure in a red coat demonstrating a glowing orrery to an audience in a dimly lit room, with children peering through the apparatus, rendered in chiaroscuro lighting. This visual aligns with the narrator's discussion of the Enlightenment, which placed 'reason over superstition and science over religion'.

Charles III as a Boy Painting.description: A portrait of a young boy with curly blonde hair, wearing a blue and gold robe, holding a small white flower and resting his hand on an open book decorated with flowers, set against a dark background with a parrot and floral arrangement. The narrator identifies him as 'the beloved Charles III,' a king who believed in religious reform and a constitutional monarchy, aligning with Enlightenment principles.

Thomas Jefferson Painting.description: A solemn portrait of Thomas Jefferson with white hair, wearing a dark coat and white cravat, gazing directly at the viewer against a dark, warm-toned background.

The Family of the Infante Don Luis (Deta.description: A detail showing a woman in a red dress holding a child, with another figure in dark clothing beside them, rendered in warm, rich tones and soft lighting.

Newton Painting.description: A mythic depiction of a nude, muscular man with curly hair, draped in white cloth, drawing geometric figures on a stone tablet in a rocky, natural landscape, rendered in earthy tones. This visual aligns with the narrator's discussion of the Enlightenment, which placed 'reason over superstition and science over religion'.

Two Old Men Painting.description: A somber oil painting of two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger, painted in rough, expressive strokes between 1819–1823.

No se puede mirar Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching showing a contorted, agonized figure with heavy cross-hatching, explicitly named and translated by the narrator as 'No se puede mirar' (It Cannot Be Looked At), emphasizing its unwatchable nature.

This is Worse Etching.description: An etching from 1864 depicting a man in despair clutching his head, with another figure behind him holding a knife, rendered in expressive, dark lines.

The Game of Horse and Rider Painting.description: A cheerful, light-toned oil painting from 1792 showing children playing, one riding on another’s shoulders, set against a pale, airy background.

They Do Not Want To Etching.description: An etching from 1864 showing a man being attacked while another figure raises a knife, rendered in stark, expressive lines, emphasizing violence and despair.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere.

Yard With Lunatics (Corral de Locos) Pai.description: A dark, haunting oil painting from 1794 depicting agitated, half-naked figures in a dimly lit, confined space. A male narrator describes it as 'a scene of violence occurring in an open courtyard of an insane asylum,' where 'the inmates are shrouded in darkness' despite the open sky, and notes its dark subject matter 'would prefigure his later etchings.'

The Parasol Painting.description: A bright, pastoral oil painting from 1777 showing a woman in a blue and yellow dress holding a green parasol, accompanied by a man in a brown jacket, set against a serene landscape, noted by the narrator as having been painted 'just before Goya was struck deaf'.

Etching Tool and Metal Plate.description: A close-up of hands using a fine etching tool to incise lines into a metal plate, illustrating the physical labor and precision of the intaglio process, which the narrator describes as 'composing the surface of the plate' and where lines are 'etched and so exposed by the artist.'

Goya or the Hard Way to the Enlightenmen.description: A film clip (1971, directed by Konrad Wolf) showing hands meticulously etching lines into a metal plate with a fine tool, illustrating the physical process of Goya’s technique.

Wooded Landscape Etching.description: A serene, detailed etching by Thomas Gainsborough (1797) of trees, a cow, and a river, rendered with fine lines and soft shading, contrasting with earlier violent scenes, and set to somber classical string music.

Intaglio.description: A close-up of an etched surface with the word 'Intaglio' overlaid, indicating the printmaking technique used, characterized by incised lines in a metal plate. The narrator details its variations, explaining how drawings are 'etched into soft waxy ground' for a delicate, intricate, and spontaneous line, or 'directly to metal' for engraving, and how the acid 'bites' into exposed lines to create grooves for ink.

The Disparate General Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, showing a dense, violent pile of figures in struggle, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, accompanied by somber classical string music.

And They are Like Wild Beasts Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, portraying frenzied, animalistic figures in combat, emphasizing brutality and despair, accompanied by somber classical string music.

Ecce Homo (Copper Plate).description: The copper plate version of Jan Norblin’s 'Ecce Homo' (1770s), showing the engraved lines and textures of the metal surface. The narrator explains that 'the etching is done back to front... as the final print will be the reversed image' of this plate.

Brush for Cleaning Plate.description: A stiff-bristled brush used to scrub the metal plate after etching, removing residue and revealing the engraved image beneath the resist layer.

Engraving Tool Tip.description: A close-up of the fine, pointed tip of the etching tool, showing its precision and the way it cuts into the metal surface, with a yellowish grip and metallic shaft.

Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Capr.description: A profile self-portrait of Francisco Goya from 1799, rendered in fine etching lines, shown alongside its copper plate. The narrator explains that 'the etching is done back to front... as the final print will be the reversed image' of the plate.

Acid Bath.description: A steaming, dark liquid bath into which the engraved plate is submerged. The narrator explains its function: 'the acid doesn't bite' where wax protects the plate, but 'attacks' exposed lines, 'leaving their design embedded in the surface in grooves which will hold ink,' and that 'gradations of line tone can be achieved by varying the length of time in the acid bath,' with 'longer periods produc[ing] more deeply bitten areas.'

Ecce Homo (Print).description: A black-and-white print by Jan Norblin (1770s) depicting a dramatic religious scene, rendered in fine etching lines, shown alongside its copper plate. The narrator explains that 'the etching is done back to front... as the final print will be the reversed image' of the plate.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a series of classical portraits, beginning with 'Charles III as a Boy' and transitioning to 'Charles III' as an adult, as a male narrator introduces him as "the beloved Charles III," a king who championed religious reform and a constitutional monarchy, embodying Enlightenment principles. While a detail from Goya's 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis' appears, the narrator elaborates on the Enlightenment as an intellectual movement that prioritized "reason over superstition and science over religion." This is visually echoed by 'Newton' intently drawing geometric figures and 'A Philosopher Lecturing on the Orrery' dramatically demonstrating a glowing scientific apparatus to an attentive audience. A solemn portrait of 'Thomas Jefferson' is then shown, followed by 'Ferdinand VII' in a formal pose. The narrator's tone shifts as he describes Ferdinand VII, who "declared himself absolute monarch," rejected modernization, "reinstated the Inquisition," and consolidated church power, revoking the constitution and clamping down on dissent with an "iron fist," dragging Spain "back into the Dark Ages." This oppressive narrative is powerfully underscored by 'The Inquisition Tribunal' painting, depicting a dark, crowded religious trial, and then by grainy black-and-white archival footage of a chaotic outdoor scene with figures running and falling amidst debris. The segment concludes with a stark black-and-white etching, '2. Dark Goya (1792 onwards)', illustrating a violent, grotesque struggle, as the narrator explains Goya's focus on "the monstrosities of war and human behavior," all set against a newly introduced somber classical string melody.

The segment opens with a close-up of Francisco Goya’s stark black-and-white etching, 'No se puede mirar' (It Cannot Be Looked At), depicting a grotesque, agonized figure, as a male narrator explicitly states the title and translates it, emphasizing that 'one cannot look at this.' The narration continues, explaining how some of Goya's work remains 'hard to look at' and how his deafness fostered a 'more experimental vein,' allowing him freedom beyond patron demands, even if his 'darker work' was censored. This context frames the subsequent etchings like 'This is Worse,' showing a man in despair with a knife-wielding figure, and the somber oil painting 'Two Old Men,' featuring emaciated figures. A shift occurs with the bright, pastoral 'The Parasol,' which the narrator notes was painted 'just before Goya was struck deaf,' followed by the subtle sound of a quill pen scratching. The mood then darkens again with the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath' and the violent etching 'They Do Not Want To,' as the narrator explains that works like these were painted 'after' his deafness, and from 1792 on, Goya's 'private world would be stripped of color and light.' A cheerful 'The Game of Horse and Rider' briefly appears before the final, extended scene: 'Yard With Lunatics (Corral de Locos).' This dark, haunting oil painting of agitated figures in a confined space is highlighted by the narrator, who reveals Goya saw such scenes in the asylum of Zaragoza, all while a somber, classical string piece plays in the background, underscoring the segment's exploration of Goya's evolving, often disturbing, artistic vision.

The video begins with a dark, chaotic oil painting, 'Yard With Lunatics (Corral de Locos) Painting,' depicting agitated, half-naked figures in a dimly lit, confined space, some embracing, others screaming or gesturing wildly, with a single light source from a barred window illuminating the chaos. A male narrator explains it 'shows a scene of violence occurring in an open courtyard of an insane asylum,' where 'the inmates are shrouded in darkness' despite the open sky, and notes that this dark subject matter 'would prefigure his later etchings.' Somber, classical string music then begins as the scene transitions to a stark black-and-white etching titled 'The Disparate General' by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, showing a dense, violent pile of figures in struggle. This is followed by a blurred transition to another Goya etching, 'And They are Like Wild Beasts,' also from 1864, portraying frenzied, animalistic figures in combat. The narrator highlights 'Goya's series of aquatint prints, Los Caprichos and The Disasters of War,' as 'one of the supreme monuments of European art.' The video then introduces the term 'Intaglio' over a close-up of an etched surface, with the narrator defining it as 'the name for any technique in which the image is cut into the surface,' where 'the cut line or sunken area holds ink.' He adds that 'achieving aquatint tones is a very complex printing process.' The scene changes to 'Wooded Landscape' by Thomas Gainsborough (1797), a serene, detailed etching, before shifting to a film clip from 'Goya or the Hard Way to the Enlightenment' (1971, directed by Konrad Wolf). This clip shows hands meticulously etching lines into a metal plate with a fine tool, illustrating the physical process as the narrator describes it: 'For the initial design, a copper plate is heated, then it is covered with a waxy, acid-resistant ground. Then, a design is drawn onto the wax ground with an etching needle.' The final frames focus on the hands continuing to work on the plate, emphasizing the precision and labor involved, all set to the continuing classical string music.

The segment opens with a close-up of hands meticulously engraving fine lines into a metal plate using a sharp etching tool, a process the narrator describes as "composing the surface of the plate." This labor-intensive technique, 'Engraving directly on to metal,' is then contrasted in a split-screen with 'Engraving on soft ground,' where, as the narrator explains, drawings are etched into a soft waxy ground rather than directly onto the metal. An expressive etching of a figure raising arms exemplifies the soft ground technique, characterized by the narrator as producing a "clean, delicate, intricate, and spontaneous" line, akin to drawing or watercolor. Historical prints, including Jan Norblin's 'Ecce Homo' (1770s) and a self-portrait etching by Goya, are shown side-by-side with their copper plates, illustrating the narrator's point that "the etching is done back to front, including all the writing... as the final print will be the reversed image." The process continues with a hand submerging a metal plate into a steaming acid bath, as the narrator states, "Once the design is etched, the plate is immersed in a bath of acid." He elaborates that areas protected by wax remain untouched, but "the lines that were etched and so exposed by the artist get attacked by the acid," leaving the design "embedded in the surface in grooves which will hold ink." A hand then scrubs the plate with a brush to reveal the etched image. The final frames show a close-up comparison of an original etching and its copper plate, emphasizing intricate lines and textures, with the narrator concluding that "gradations of line tone can be achieved by varying the length of time in the acid bath," as "longer periods produce more deeply bitten areas."

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit environment, a hand uses a torch to reveal aged wooden planks, setting the stage for a printmaking explanation. The process begins with covering a plate with ink, pushing it into grooves, and lifting most of the ink, leaving the grooves filled. A sepia-toned etching, 'Three Stages of Aquatint' by Julius Caesar Ibbetson, is shown, illustrating the initial engraving before aquatint tones are added to create tonal effects rather than lines. Paper is soaked, placed on the inked plate, and run through a press. In a modern studio, Roisin grinds powdered rosin, an acid-resistant substance, in a mortar and pestle. She applies this ground to a copper plate with a brush, which is then heated and immersed in an acid bath, similar to etching, where the acid etches around the dust particles. The resulting copper plate exhibits a fine, granular texture, demonstrating successful aquatint tonal areas. A side-by-side comparison visually transforms a stark line drawing into a tonal, textured image with added depth, with the narrator explaining aquatint creates a "grainy sandpaper-like surface" holding ink for a "wash" effect. A detailed etching by Ibbetson, depicting a pastoral scene, is shown. Melancholic classical music begins as Francisco Goya’s 1799 etching 'Can’t Anyone Unleash Us? (Plate 75),' showing a woman under an owl, is presented. A historical newspaper, 'Diario de Madrid' from December 16, 1836, is displayed, noting an ad from February 6, 1799, where Goya announced new etchings, including a quote about his social critique and a disclaimer. A grid of Goya’s 'Los Caprichos' etchings, a series of 80 etchings and aquatints, is shown, with the narrator explaining Goya used them to depict "the hypocrisies and follies of an unenlightened population" and critique "the vanity and ignorance of contemporary society." This collection includes 'Bon Voyage (Plate 64),' a chaotic, nightmarish scene of distressed figures rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic shading, evoking horror and despair. Close-ups of 'Bon Voyage' highlight expressive, agitated faces and a dark, oppressive atmosphere, as the narrator begins to describe how aquatint allowed artists to create a watercolor effect.

Brush for Cleaning Plate.description: A stiff-bristled brush used to scrub the metal plate after etching, removing residue and revealing the engraved image beneath the resist layer; shown being used to apply ground to a copper plate before etching.

Engraving Tool Tip.description: A close-up of the fine, pointed tip of the etching tool, showing its precision and the way it cuts into the metal surface, with a yellowish grip and metallic shaft; not directly visible, but implied by the application of ground and etching process.

Acid Bath.description: A steaming, dark liquid bath, into which the copper plate is immersed, where the acid 'eats into the metal around the dust particles' to create tonal areas, as described by the narrator.

Yellowish Ground Substance.description: A fine, powdery, yellowish material, labeled 'Roisin' on-screen, shown being ground and applied to the copper plate; the narrator identifies it as 'powdered rosin,' an 'acid-resistant material' used to create tonal areas on the plate.

Etching Tool and Metal Plate.description: A close-up of hands using a fine etching tool to incise lines into a metal plate, shown in the film clip, highlighting the physical labor and precision of the intaglio process; not directly shown, but the process of applying ground and etching implies its use.

Mortar and Pestle.description: A white ceramic mortar and wooden pestle used by Roisin to grind a yellowish substance, which the narrator identifies as 'powdered rosin,' an acid-resistant material.

Copper Plate.description: A rectangular, metallic plate, initially smooth and shiny, later covered in a granular texture after being submerged in acid; described by the narrator as being 'covered with ink,' 'inked-up,' 'dusted with an acid-resistant material called powdered rosin,' and 'immersed in an acid bath' to create tonal effects.

Roisin.description: A person, identified by on-screen text, shown in a modern studio grinding a yellowish substance in a mortar and applying it to a copper plate; she is preparing what the narrator calls 'powdered rosin' for the aquatint process.

Three Stages of Aquatint.description: A sepia-toned etching by Julius Caesar Ibbetson (1794) depicting a pastoral scene with a donkey carrying a family, a child leading it, and other figures in the background, rendered in fine, expressive lines; shown being examined and then referenced as a historical example of aquatint technique, which a male narrator explains creates a "grainy sandpaper-like surface" that holds ink to give the effect of an "area of wash."

Intaglio.description: A close-up of an etched surface with the word 'Intaglio' overlaid, indicating the printmaking technique used in the etchings shown, characterized by incised lines in a metal plate; implied by the process shown, though not explicitly labeled in this segment.

Los Caprichos (1799) Etchings.description: A collection of 80 satirical etchings by Francisco Goya, displayed in a grid format, depicting grotesque, absurd, and often dark scenes of human folly, superstition, and social corruption, rendered with expressive lines and tonal contrasts. A male narrator identifies them as "Los Caprichos, or The Caprices," explaining Goya used them to show "the hypocrisies and follies of an unenlightened population" and critique "the vanity and ignorance of contemporary society."

Bon Voyage (Plate 64) Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) from the 'Los Caprichos' series, showing a chaotic, nightmarish scene of figures in distress, with a central figure being attacked or consumed by others, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic shading, evoking a sense of horror and despair, and thematically described by a male narrator as part of Goya's critique of societal hypocrisy and folly.

Diario de Madrid.description: A historical newspaper from December 16, 1836, featuring official announcements and legal notices in Spanish, with a quote overlay. A male narrator states that on February 6, 1799, this newspaper carried an ad from Francisco Goya announcing the publication and sale of a series of new etchings, quoting Goya's disclaimer that "In none of the compositions that form this collection has the author proposed to ridicule the particular faults of one or another individual."

Can’t Anyone Unleash Us? (Plate 75) Etch.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) showing a woman in a state of distress, struggling under the weight of a large, menacing owl perched on her back, symbolizing oppression or ignorance, with expressive lines and dramatic shading, accompanied by melancholic classical music.

The copper plate is shown submerged in the steaming acid bath, gently agitated, before the final shot reveals its surface with a fine, granular texture, demonstrating the successful creation of aquatint tonal areas.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with Francisco Goya’s etching *Here Comes the Bogeyman (Plate 3)*, illustrating a monstrous figure and a terrified child, as a narrator discusses Goya's aquatint technique and the 'shadowy world of ignorance.' The visual shifts to Goya’s *Self-Portrait (Plate 1)*, then to his portrait of *Charles IV and Family*, with the narrator noting Charles IV's reign was marked by 'crisis after crisis.' Robert Cleveley's *Battle of Cape Vincent (1797)*, a naval battle scene, plays as distant battle sounds accompany the narration of Spain's economic weakening due to wars with revolutionary France and England. A chaotic crowd scene print, possibly depicting social upheaval, is shown while the narrator mentions government censorship of the press. A satirical cartoon of King Charles IV and his family in infantile forms illustrates the flourishing of 'social satire like Los Caprichos.' Goya's *The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43)* etching, featuring a sleeping man surrounded by owls and bats, is presented with its Spanish inscription, and the narrator explains Goya's interest in the Enlightenment after his deafness. Léon Cogniet's painting *The French Revolution*, depicting a revolutionary crowd with soldiers and smoke, concludes the segment, with faint crowd murmurs accompanying the narrator's attribution of inspiration to Jean-Jacques Rousseau. The narrative continues with a close-up of Jean-Jacques Rousseau's portrait, his quote 'Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains' appearing on screen, as the narrator mentions words 'censored by both the Spanish church and the Crown.' A 1793 edition of 'Philosophie' is shown, its frontispiece resembling Goya's etching and potentially 'sowing the seeds for Goya's own personal enlightenment.' Goya's etching *The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43)* is detailed, with the narrator identifying the sleeping man as 'Goya himself' and explaining the 'struggle between reason and imagination,' with owls and bats symbolizing 'ignorance and evil,' and a cat 'watching vigilantly.' An owl is depicted 'handing over a brush' to the sleeping man. The segment concludes with a split-screen of the etching and Goya's profile self-portrait, labeled 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor.' The narrative then focuses on Goya's *Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Caprichos)* alongside *The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43)*, the latter symbolizing 'what happens when reason or rationality is asleep' and 'superstitious beliefs take over from science and reason,' leading to 'monsters, or monstrous ideas,' accompanied by dramatic orchestral music. A close-up of *The Bewitched Man* painting and the etching *Out Hunting for Teeth* are shown, followed by a rapid sequence of Los Caprichos etchings: *A Man with a Winged Monster*, *A Girl with a Sword*, *A Frightened Man*, and *And His House is on Fire*. The narrator explains that 'in Los Caprichos, no member of Spanish society was spared his acid pen,' targeting 'superstitious peasants and prostitutes,' 'belief in witches,' 'the ignorance of the ruling classes,' and 'most shockingly of all, the Catholic Church,' noting Goya's 'particularly dangerous' satire of religion. The final etching, *No one has seen us (Plate 79)*, depicting three men drinking beside a barrel with the Spanish text 'Nadie nos ha visto,' illustrates the narrator's description of 'a group of clergymen getting drunk besides an enormous barrel of beer,' further highlighting Goya's dangerous satire of religion, which included scenes like 'a drunken priest pulling up his trousers after consummating an unspecified sexual act.'

The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (P.description: A black-and-white etching of a sleeping man surrounded by owls, bats, and a cat, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos', which the male narrator states 'has come to symbolize the overall meaning of the series,' representing 'what happens when reason or rationality is asleep' and 'superstitious beliefs take over from science and reason,' leading to 'monsters, or monstrous ideas.'

The French Revolution Painting.description: A large, dynamic painting by Léon Cogniet (1792) depicting a revolutionary crowd with soldiers, horses, and smoke, capturing the chaos and fervor of the French Revolution, as the narrator explains Jean-Jacques Rousseau's words inspired the French revolutionaries, accompanied by faint crowd murmurs.

Battle of Cape Vincent (1797) Painting.description: A dramatic naval battle scene by Robert Cleveley (1800) depicting ships engaged in combat under a stormy sky, with smoke and waves, capturing the chaos of war at sea, visually aligning with the narrator's mention of "wars with revolutionary France and then England" that weakened Spain's economy, accompanied by distant battle sounds.

Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Capr.description: A profile self-portrait of Francisco Goya (1799) wearing a tall black hat and formal coat, with the inscription 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor' beneath, rendered in fine etching lines, identified by the male narrator as 'the self-portrait he went with' for the series' title page.

Satirical Cartoon of Charles IV and Fami.description: A colorful political cartoon depicting King Charles IV and his family in exaggerated, infantile forms, with labels 'Prince of Asturias' and 'Imperial Grandee', satirizing their incompetence and indulgence, which the narrator highlights as an example of how "social satire like Los Caprichos flourished" despite government censorship.

Here Comes the Bogeyman (Plate 3) Etchin.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) showing a monstrous, shadowy figure looming over a terrified child, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, evoking fear and the supernatural, which the narrator describes as revealing the "shadowy world of ignorance" through Goya's mastery of aquatints.

Charles IV and Family Painting.description: A formal, richly colored portrait by Francisco Goya (1800) of the Spanish royal family in opulent attire, with Goya’s self-portrait subtly visible in the background, highlighting his role as court painter, during a period the narrator states was dominated by "crisis after crisis" under Charles IV's reign.

Crowd Scene Print.description: A chaotic, colorful print of a crowd in 18th-century clothing, possibly depicting a political or social upheaval, with figures holding papers and gesturing wildly, suggesting unrest or protest.

Jean-Jacques Rousseau Painting.description: A classical portrait of Jean-Jacques Rousseau by François Guerin (1760), showing him with powdered white hair, a serene expression, and formal 18th-century clothing, including a white cravat and dark coat, set against a dark background.

Philosophie (1793 Edition).description: An aged book with a frontispiece engraving titled 'PHILOSOPHY,' depicting a philosopher at a desk, surrounded by symbolic figures, books, and a draped banner, rendered in fine black-and-white etching lines on yellowed paper. The narrator specifically identifies it as 'Rousseau's 1793 edition of philosophy' and suggests it 'sowed the seeds for Goya's own personal enlightenment,' noting its striking resemblance to Goya's etching.

The Bewitched Man.description: A dark, oil painting (1798) showing a man in a black cloak recoiling in terror from a monstrous, horned figure in a starry night, with expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere.

No one has seen us (Plate 79).description: An etching (1799) depicting three men drinking from mugs beside a barrel, with the handwritten Spanish text 'Nadie nos ha visto' circled at the bottom, visually aligning with the male narrator's description of 'a group of clergymen getting drunk besides an enormous barrel of beer,' and fitting the broader context of Goya's satire on clerical misconduct, including a 'drunken priest pulling up his trousers' as mentioned by the narrator.

Out Hunting for Teeth.description: An etching (1799) depicting a man pulling teeth from another’s mouth, rendered with stark, expressive lines, shown with a fiery background scene of a burning pyre and silhouetted figures, evoking persecution.

And His House is on Fire (Plate 18).description: An etching (1799) showing a man laughing as a chair burns beside him, with the Spanish inscription 'Y se le quema la Casa' at the bottom, symbolizing ironic or self-destructive consequences.

A Girl with a Sword (Plate 56).description: An etching depicting a woman in a flowing dress brandishing a sword over a man on the ground, with a crowd in the background, rendered in dynamic, expressive lines, symbolizing violence or justice.

A Frightened Man (Plate 81).description: An etching showing a man with arms raised in terror, being attacked by another figure, with a third figure in the background, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, evoking fear and chaos.

A Man with a Winged Monster (Plate 54).description: An etching showing a man being attacked by a winged, demonic figure, rendered with dramatic shading and expressive lines, symbolizing supernatural or societal terror.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a close-up of Francisco Goya’s black-and-white etching *Here Comes the Bogeyman (Plate 3)*, depicting a monstrous figure looming over a terrified child, as a male narrator describes Goya's mastery of aquatints to reveal a "shadowy world of ignorance." The visual transitions to Goya’s *Self-Portrait (Plate 1)*, showing him in a tall black hat, then to his grand, colorful portrait of *Charles IV and Family*, with Goya subtly visible, while the narrator explains Charles IV's reign was dominated by "crisis after crisis." Distant battle sounds accompany Robert Cleveley's *Battle of Cape Vincent (1797)*, a dramatic naval scene, as the narrator details "wars with revolutionary France and then England" that weakened Spain's economy. The video returns to Goya’s *The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43)*, an etching of a man asleep surrounded by owls and bats with its Spanish inscription visible, as the narrator explicitly names it and explains Goya's personal interest in the Enlightenment after becoming deaf. A subtle, eerie background sound underscores the historical and artistic narrative throughout.

The minute opens with a close-up of Jean-Jacques Rousseau's classical portrait, painted by François Guerin in 1760, depicting him with powdered white hair and formal 18th-century attire, as his famous quote, 'Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains,' gradually appears on screen. A male narrator, speaking with a formal British accent, provides context, mentioning words that were "censored by both the Spanish church and the Crown," setting a historical and political backdrop. The scene then transitions to a historical 1793 edition of 'Philosophie,' whose engraved frontispiece, showing a philosopher at a desk surrounded by symbolic figures and books, is highlighted. The narrator points out its "striking resemblance to Goya's etching," suggesting this book might have "sowed the seeds for Goya's own personal enlightenment." The video then shifts to a detailed view of Francisco Goya's etching, 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters' (Plate 43), which shows a man slumped asleep at a desk, surrounded by ominous owls, bats, and a cat, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' clearly visible. The narrator identifies the "distraught man slumped over the desk" as "Goya himself," citing an earlier preparatory sketch. As the camera zooms in on the etching's intricate details, highlighting the owls, bats, and the sleeping man's face and hands, the narrator explains that Goya depicts "the struggle between reason and imagination," noting that his contemporary audience would have recognized the "menacing bats and owls as symbols of ignorance and evil." The zoom continues, revealing a lynx (cat) at the lower right, which the narrator describes as "watching vigilantly," alerting viewers to "the rise of monstrous forces that appear when sleep descends." The camera then focuses on the tools of the trade—paper and brushes—by the man's hands, as the narrator points out an owl "handing over a brush as if to encourage him to continue his work." The segment concludes with a full view of the etching, followed by a split-screen displaying the etching on the right and a profile self-portrait of Goya, labeled 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor,' on the left, visually linking the artwork to its creator, as the narrator's final words, "We know that Goya originally intended this...", are cut off.

The video opens with a split-screen, showing Goya's Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Caprichos) on the left, which a male narrator identifies as "the self-portrait he went with" for the series' title page, alongside The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43) Etching on the right. This etching, featuring a sleeping man surrounded by owls, bats, and a cat with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos', is described by the narrator as symbolizing "the overall meaning of the series," explaining "what happens when reason or rationality is asleep" and "superstitious beliefs take over from science and reason," leading to "monsters, or monstrous ideas." A dramatic, slightly ominous orchestral music swells as this explanation concludes. The scene transitions to a close-up of The Bewitched Man painting, depicting a man recoiling in terror from a monstrous, horned figure, followed by the etching Out Hunting for Teeth, showing a man pulling teeth with a fiery background suggesting persecution. The video then returns to the Self-Portrait, zooming in on Goya’s profile. A rapid sequence of etchings from Los Caprichos follows, including A Man with a Winged Monster, A Girl with a Sword, A Frightened Man, and And His House is on Fire, each depicting grotesque, satirical, or violent scenes. During this sequence, the narrator explains that "in Los Caprichos, no member of Spanish society was spared his acid pen," listing "superstitious peasants and prostitutes," "belief in witches," "the ignorance of the ruling classes," and "most shockingly of all, the Catholic Church" as targets, emphasizing that "Goya's satire of religion was particularly dangerous." The final etching shown is No one has seen us (Plate 79), depicting three men drinking from mugs beside a barrel with the handwritten Spanish text 'Nadie nos ha visto' circled at the bottom, emphasizing secrecy and complicity. This visual directly corresponds to the narrator's description of "a group of clergymen getting drunk besides an enormous barrel of beer," further illustrating Goya's dangerous satire of religion, which also included scenes like "a drunken priest pulling up his trousers after consummating an unspecified sexual act," as mentioned by the narrator.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a dark, red-tinted close-up from the film *Haxan*, followed by bound hands, as a narrator states, "no one has seen us." This transitions to Francisco Goya’s paintings, including *Men Reading* with its shadowed figures, the chaotic *Night Scene From the Inquisition*, and *The Inquisition Tribunal*, a dimly lit courtroom, while the narrator explains the "religious oppression in Spain" by the Inquisition until 1834, which dictated reading materials and persecuted dissenters. A split-screen shows Goya’s self-portrait beside a monstrous etching, revealing Goya was "reported to the authorities" and "withdrew the Caprichos from public sale." The scene shifts to the modern San Fernando Fine Art Royal Academy, displaying a printing press and etchings, where the narrator notes Goya gifted his original copper plates to the King, who then gave them to the Academy. A clip from *Reign of Terror* depicts a frenzied crowd during the "political revolution" in France that "devolved into violence," leading to "Napoleon becoming emperor of France," accompanied by Ingres's portrait of Napoleon on his imperial throne. The video then shows a portrait of Joseph Bonaparte, symbolizing imperial authority, as the narrator details the consequences for Spain. Gunshots and a horse whinny precede Goya's 'The Second of May 1808,' depicting "spontaneous rioting" and "Spanish citizens rose up against the French occupiers" on May 2nd, 1808. This is followed by 'The Third of May 1808,' showing an execution where "hundreds of Spanish citizens were rounded up and shot" the next day, serving as a symbol for "all the brutalities of war," reinforced by archival footage of a similar execution. Goya's 'Self-Portrait' and 'The French General Guye' painting are shown as the narrator discusses Goya's role as court painter for the occupying Napoleonic court, witnessing the war's horrors while working in secret on "new etchings which would be called the disasters of war," illustrated by 'Bury Them and Keep Quiet,' 'What is This Hubbub?,' and 'For a Clasp Knife.' The segment concludes with a split-screen of 'The Third of May 1808' and 'With or Without Reason,' noting that "some of the ideas from the etchings would inform his Third of May canvas."

San Fernando Fine Art Royal Academy.description: A modern museum gallery with polished floors and wooden walls, featuring a large wooden printing press in the center and framed etchings displayed along the walls, illuminated by warm lighting, explicitly named by the narrator as 'the San Fernando Academy of Fine Arts' where Goya's original copper plates were gifted and 'remain today'.

Reign of Terror.description: A black-and-white film clip (1949, directed by Anthony Mann) showing a frenzied crowd in revolutionary fervor, raising their arms and weapons, with visible text identifying the film and director, accompanied by the distinct sound of a 'frenzied crowd, shouting,' as the narrator describes it as a 'political revolution' that 'devolved into violence'.

Night Scene From the Inquisition.description: A fiery, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya (1810) depicting a night of persecution, with figures in torment under a blazing sky, rendered in dramatic, expressive brushwork, as the narrator details the Inquisition's power and the persecution of those who went against its teachings.

Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Capr.description: A profile etching of Francisco Goya (1799) wearing a tall black hat and formal coat, with the inscription 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor' beneath, shown in split-screen with a monstrous figure, while the narrator explains that Goya was 'reported to the authorities' and 'withdrew the Caprichos from public sale' due to persecution.

A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men! (Plate 39).description: A detailed etching by Francisco Goya (1863) showing a muscular, horned figure dragging a corpse through a rocky landscape, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic shading, shown in split-screen with the self-portrait, as the narrator explains Goya's persecution and withdrawal of his 'Caprichos' from public sale.

Napoleon on His Imperial Throne.description: A grand, formal portrait by Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres (1806) of Napoleon seated on a golden throne, wearing a white ermine robe, red sash, and crown, holding a scepter and orb, with a stern, authoritative expression, which the narrator identifies as the outcome of the revolution, 'leading to Napoleon becoming emperor of France'.

Men Reading (From the Black Paintings).description: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819–1823) showing a group of men huddled around a single light source, their faces distorted and shadowed, evoking despair and isolation, while the narrator discusses the 'religious oppression in Spain' and the Inquisition's control over what 'could be read'.

The Inquisition Tribunal.description: A dimly lit, crowded oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819) showing a religious trial with figures in conical hats, illuminated by a single candle, emphasizing oppression and judgment, while the narrator explains how the church had 'complete control' and decided 'what art was and wasn't acceptable'.

Haxan.description: A 1922 silent film by Benjamin Christensen, shown in a red-tinted close-up of a man’s face, conveying psychological torment or ecstasy, with visible text identifying the film and director, over which a male narrator begins speaking about unseen individuals.

Archival Footage.description: Grainy black-and-white footage depicting a chaotic outdoor scene with soldiers in a firing squad execution, reinforcing the historical context of 'The Third of May 1808'.

The Third of May 1808.description: A powerful oil painting by Francisco Goya (1814) showing a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance, audibly identified as depicting one of the executions where 'hundreds of Spanish citizens were rounded up and shot' on 'the next day' (May 3rd), serving as a symbol for 'all the brutalities of war,' and later noted as being informed by Goya's etching ideas.

Joseph Bonaparte Painting.description: A full-length portrait of Joseph Bonaparte seated on a golden throne, wearing opulent white and gold royal attire with a red cloak, holding a scepter and orb, set against a dark background, symbolizing imperial authority.

Self-Portrait Painting.description: A painted self-portrait of Francisco Goya (1815) with dark, curly hair, a weary expression, and a dark coat, set against a dark background, reflecting his later, introspective phase.

Bury Them and Keep Quiet Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man standing over a pile of naked, lifeless bodies, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, audibly part of Goya's 'new etchings which would be called the disasters of war,' created in secret due to witnessing war horrors.

For a Clasp Knife Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man seated on a pile of heads, holding a knife, with a crowd below, rendered in stark, expressive lines, symbolizing violence and absurdity, audibly part of Goya's 'new etchings which would be called the disasters of war,' created in secret due to witnessing war horrors.

With or Without Reason Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (1819–1823) depicting a woman holding a child as a soldier attacks, rendered in expressive lines, emphasizing violence and helplessness, audibly part of Goya's 'new etchings which would be called the disasters of war,' created in secret due to witnessing war horrors, and explicitly linked to informing the 'Third of May canvas.'

The Second of May 1808 Painting.description: A chaotic battle scene by Francisco Goya (1814) depicting Spanish civilians and French soldiers in violent struggle, with horses rearing and figures in motion, rendered in earthy tones and dynamic composition, audibly illustrating the 'spontaneous rioting' on 'May the 2nd, 1808' where 'Spanish citizens rose up against the French occupiers,' accompanied by a gunshot and horse whinny.

What is This Hubbub? Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a man in distress clutching his head, rendered with expressive, dark lines, evoking despair and confusion, audibly part of Goya's 'new etchings which would be called the disasters of war,' created in secret due to witnessing war horrors.

The French General Guye Painting.description: A formal portrait by Francisco Goya (1810) of a French officer in a richly decorated military uniform with gold epaulettes, a red sash, and medals, symbolizing authority and power.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a black-and-white triptych from Abel Gance’s *Napoléon*, establishing a historical and cinematic tone as a narrator explains the period of Napoleon's ousting and Ferdinand VII's return. Transitioning to a dimly lit museum gallery, the narrator details how Goya was commissioned to paint "the Spanish rebellion against the French" and "the execution of those Spanish heroes by Napoleonic troops." A close-up of Goya's *The Third of May 1808* reveals a man in a white shirt before a firing squad, with the narrator clarifying the event occurred six years prior and that Goya, facing questions about his loyalty, completed the paintings "extremely quickly, within a couple of months," as he "had a lot to prove." Somber classical string music begins as outlines highlight soldiers and victims in the painting, and a split-screen comparison shows its original composition. The narrator identifies the artwork as "Third of May 1808," describing "two groups of men," with "the anonymous firing squad of the French army" depicted as "a single unit, an instrument of war." A portrait of Ferdinand VII by José de Madrazo is shown, followed by *The Third of May 1808* with text overlays identifying it as 'The Painting,' and the narrator concludes by noting the "anonymity of oppressors" as the frames zoom in on faces. The narrative then shifts to a full view of Goya's *The Third of May 1808*, with a speaker highlighting the contrast between "passion of the pleading victims" and the "cold mechanical firing squad." The central figure, a "Christ-like figure" and "anti-hero," is identified as a "symbol of martyrdom" representing "man's inhumanity to man," with his "whiteness of his shirt" signifying "the innocence of all victims." The speaker points out the "lantern on the ground" illuminates the main figure, and a split-screen comparison of *The Third of May 1808* with Picasso’s *Guernica* reveals Picasso referencing Goya's "crucifixion pose" and "wounds of the stigmata" for his "anti-war painting." The video briefly shows Caravaggio’s *The Calling of Saint Matthew*, with the speaker discussing how "lights, candles, and lanterns" in Christian art symbolize "the presence of God" in martyrdom, before returning to a full view of Goya's *The Third of May 1808* for further elaboration.

The Painting.description: A text overlay identifying the central artwork as 'The Painting' during a close-up of *The Third of May 1808*, reinforcing the male narrator's focus on this specific artwork, which he describes as one of two 'enormous history paintings' Goya created to prove his loyalty.

Ferdinand VII Painting.description: A formal portrait of King Ferdinand VII, painted in 1821 by José de Madrazo, visually depicting him in a white military uniform with a gold-embellished sash and high white cravat, on a white horse. The male narrator mentions Ferdinand VII was 'on his way back in' after Napoleon's departure, providing historical context for his reign.

The Third of May 1808.description: A powerful oil painting by Francisco Goya (1814) showing a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance; shown in full, with close-ups and outlines emphasizing the soldiers and victims. A male speaker describes its intensity, highlighting the contrast between the “passion of the pleading victims” and the “cold mechanical firing squad.” The central figure is identified as a “Christ-like figure,” an “anti-hero” executed “like a dog,” whose “whiteness of his shirt” symbolizes the “innocence of all victims” and represents “martyrdom” and “man's inhumanity to man.” The scene's lighting is noted to come from a “lantern on the ground” specifically illuminating the main figure, whose “crucifixion pose” and “wounds of the stigmata” are later referenced by Picasso.

Museum Gallery.description: A dimly lit, spacious art gallery with polished floors and white walls, featuring large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series displayed under focused lighting, with visitors observing them in quiet contemplation.

Napoleon Painting.description: A black-and-white triptych from Abel Gance’s 1927 film *Napoléon*, showing Napoleon on horseback flanked by soldiers in a panoramic, dynamic composition, emphasizing historical grandeur and cinematic innovation.

X-Rays.description: A split-screen comparison showing the original composition of *The Third of May 1808* with a horse and rider on the left, and the final version with the firing squad on the right, revealing the painting’s evolution and hidden layers.

Outline Highlighting.description: White outlines superimposed on *The Third of May 1808* to emphasize specific figures, such as the firing squad and the central victim, drawing attention to their forms and emotional impact.

Split-Screen Comparison.description: A visual technique used to juxtapose two versions of *The Third of May 1808*—one with a horse and rider, the other with the firing squad—highlighting the painting’s compositional changes and artistic decisions.

Guernica.description: A large, monochromatic painting by Pablo Picasso (1937) depicting a chaotic scene of war and suffering, with distorted figures, a bull, and a horse, rendered in Cubist style; shown in split-screen comparison with *The Third of May 1808* to highlight thematic parallels. A male speaker identifies it as Picasso's “anti-war painting,” which references Goya's *The Third of May 1808* for the “crucifixion pose” and “wounds of the stigmata” of its central figure, as well as the “pose of the central figure.”

The Calling of Saint Matthew.description: A dramatic Baroque painting by Caravaggio (1599) showing a beam of light illuminating a group of men in a dark room, with one figure pointing toward another, symbolizing divine calling; shown in full with strong chiaroscuro lighting.

raw_segments: The video opens with a black-and-white triptych from Abel Gance’s 1927 silent film *Napoléon*, showing Napoleon on horseback flanked by soldiers, establishing a historical and cinematic tone. A male narrator begins, explaining that this period was "when Napoleon was driven out and Ferdinand VII was on his way back in." The scene transitions to a dimly lit museum gallery where visitors walk past large, framed paintings, as the narrator details how Goya approached the provisional government to paint "two enormous history paintings," one depicting "the Spanish rebellion against the French" and another, "the execution of those Spanish heroes by Napoleonic troops."
