Somber, classical string music begins around 0:37. The painting is shown in detail, with outlines emphasizing the soldiers and victims, and a split-screen comparison reveals its original composition with a horse and rider. The narrator explicitly names the artwork, stating "Third of May 1808 is set in the early morning after the uprising," and describes its composition: "two groups of men," with "the anonymous firing squad of the French army" in the shadows, "melded together as a single unit, an instrument of war." A formal portrait of Ferdinand VII by José de Madrazo (1821) is then displayed, before returning to *The Third of May 1808* with text overlays identifying it as 'The Painting'. The narrator concludes by noting that "we cannot see their faces, emphasizing the anonymity of oppressors," as the final frames zoom in on the soldiers’ faces and the central victim, reinforcing the emotional intensity and historical gravity.

The video opens with a full view of Francisco Goya’s *The Third of May 1808*, a powerful and emotionally charged painting depicting a Spanish civilian being executed by a French firing squad under a dark, moonlit sky. A male speaker immediately highlights the intense contrast between the “passion of the pleading victims” and the “cold mechanical firing squad.” As the camera slowly zooms in on the central figure, a man in a white shirt and yellow trousers with arms raised, the speaker identifies him as a “Christ-like figure,” an “anti-hero” about to be executed “on the side of the road like a dog,” with “bodies of slaughtered citizens” piling up beneath him. The speaker explains Goya uses this figure as a “symbol of martyrdom,” representing “man's inhumanity to man,” and notes that the “whiteness of his shirt” is there to represent the “innocence of all victims.” The camera then pans slightly to show the firing squad, reinforcing the inevitability of death, while the speaker points out that the scene's dramatic lighting is from a “lantern on the ground” that is “actually only throwing light on the main figure.” Midway through, the video transitions to a split-screen comparison, showing *The Third of May 1808* alongside Pablo Picasso’s *Guernica*. The speaker explicitly connects Goya's central figure's “crucifixion pose” and the “wounds of the stigmata” shown on his hands as elements Picasso would “reference for Guernica,” his “anti-war painting,” along with “the pose of the central figure.” The video then cuts to Caravaggio’s *The Calling of Saint Matthew*, showing a dramatic moment of divine intervention with a beam of light, as the speaker generally discusses how “lights, candles, and lanterns” are often tied into images of “martyrdom in Christian art” as a “metaphor for the presence of God.” Finally, the video returns to *The Third of May 1808*, pulling back to the full composition, as the speaker begins to elaborate further on Goya's painting.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with a dramatic close-up of Francisco Goya’s *The Third of May 1808*, focusing on the central figure in a white shirt with raised arms before a firing squad under a dark, moonlit sky, with a narrator explaining the light's purpose for the execution and Goya's subversion of traditional art by removing God and heroic figures, instead showing a powerful state exacting revenge against ordinary citizens. The painting depicts figures, including a friar, cowering in fear as blood seeps into the sand, with the narrator emphasizing Goya's truthful portrayal of war's atrocity, punctuated by a chilling shout of "Fire!" and a gunshot sound. Brief visual transitions include Antoine-Jean Gros’s *The Battle of Abukir*, showing Napoleon on a white horse amidst chaos, and an anguished face from Goya’s painting. The scene then shifts to Miquel Parra’s *Triumphal Welcome of Ferdinand VII*, a bright, celebratory street scene with crowds and carriages under a clear blue sky, accompanied by the sounds of horse hooves and a carriage, as the narrator notes Ferdinand's return in 1813. The segment concludes with a fiery, chaotic scene of burning branches, suggesting destruction, as the audio abruptly cuts off.

Triumphal Welcome of Ferdinand VII.description: A bright, celebratory painting by Miquel Parra (1815) showing a crowded street with carriages, crowds, and buildings under a clear blue sky, symbolizing restoration and hope. The narrator mentions "Fernando was on his way back in" in 1813, aligning with the painting's subject, and the scene is accompanied by the sounds of horse hooves and a carriage.

Burning Branches Scene.description: A fiery, chaotic scene of burning branches with intense orange and red hues and rising smoke, suggesting destruction or revolution, possibly symbolic of violence or upheaval.

The Third of May 1808.description: A powerful oil painting by Francisco Goya (1814) showing a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance; shown in full, with close-ups and outlines emphasizing the soldiers and victims. A narrator extensively analyzes its use of light to highlight the atrocity, its subversion of traditional war painting by depicting no heroes and ordinary citizens cowering in fear with blood seeping into the sand, and its portrayal of truth. The painting is directly accompanied by a shouted "Fire!" and a gunshot sound.

The Battle of Abukir Painting.description: A chaotic battle scene by Antoine-Jean Gros (1807) depicting Napoleon on a white horse amidst smoke, fallen soldiers, and vibrant reds and golds, rendered in dynamic composition and dramatic lighting.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic close-up of Francisco Goya’s *The Third of May 1808*, focusing on the central figure in a white shirt with raised arms before a firing squad under a dark, moonlit sky. A narrator explains how "the light is there to make an easy shot for the firing squad and to force us to witness the atrocity," as the camera slowly zooms out to reveal the full composition. The narrator continues, stating Goya "subverts the whole concept of light and takes God out of the equation," followed by a chilling shout of "Fire!" and a gunshot sound. He elaborates that Goya "broke the rules of art by creating a war painting that has no heroes," showing "a powerful state exacting revenge against ordinary citizens," contrasting it with traditional war paintings about "opposing armies, about victory." He highlights how Goya's figures, "including a friar, are not heroic, but cower in fear as a mass of blood seeps into the sand," concluding that Goya "shows us the truth." Visually, the scene briefly transitions to Antoine-Jean Gros’s *The Battle of Abukir*, depicting Napoleon on a white horse amidst a chaotic battlefield, then cuts to an anguished face from Goya’s painting before returning to its full view. The video then shifts to Miquel Parra’s *Triumphal Welcome of Ferdinand VII*, a bright, celebratory street scene with crowds and carriages under a clear blue sky, as the narrator mentions, "In 1813, Fernando was on his way back in," accompanied by the distinct sounds of horse hooves and a carriage. The segment concludes with a fiery, chaotic scene of burning branches, suggesting destruction, as the audio cuts off.

event_blocks.summary: Grainy black-and-white archival footage of a chaotic outdoor execution, featuring a firing squad and distressed civilians, sets the stage as a narrator explains that upon King Ferdinand VII's return to Madrid in 1814, he "did not approve of the two paintings created in his absence," leading them to be "put in storage" for over 40 years, a sentiment tied to his desire to avoid reminding citizens of their power to "overthrow their oppressors." The scene transitions to a modern museum gallery where Museum Handlers install a large, dramatic painting, then moves through a long, arched Corridor with Paintings leading to a close-up of a formal portrait of King Ferdinand VII. The focus shifts to Goya's 'The Third of May 1808', identified as the benchmark for "all future paintings of violent conflict," a "powerful statement on tragedy and abuse of power in the name of liberation," where it is "perfectly clear who were the bad guys and who were the good." This is followed by a somber string-based background music and a split-screen comparison of 'The Third of May 1808' with Manet's 'The Execution of Maximilian', Picasso's 'Massacre in Korea', and Golub's 'Vietnam II', visually reinforcing the enduring theme that is "as important today as it was 200 years ago." The video then presents Goya’s 'The Disasters of War', a series of 82 aquatint etchings created between 1810 and 1819, described as "much more ambiguous" and "much more confrontational" than his black paintings, rejecting "bombastic heroics" for "reportage" that focuses on "the effects on individuals" using "shadow and shade to show its universal truth." Specific etchings like *Bury Them and Keep Quiet*, *The Women are Courageous*, *Gloomy Presentiments of What Must Come to Pass*, and *Why?* are shown, with the narrator explaining Goya "personally lived through this, seen many of these atrocities with his own eyes," directly linked to the *I Have Seen This* etching, featuring the Spanish inscription 'Yo lo vi' translated as "I have seen this." The sequence continues with *And it Cannot be Helped* and *What Good is a Cup?*, depicting scenes of mourning and starvation, as Goya is noted to have seen "people starving to death on the streets" and "executions." The video rapidly presents more etchings, with the narrator explaining Goya's refusal to take sides, portraying both Spanish and French as cruel, categorizing the works into those on the Napoleonic invasion, famine, and satire of the church and absolutism. Etchings such as *The Same*, *Cartloads for the Cemetery*, *A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men!*, *The Worst is to Beg*, *Perhaps They are Another Breed*, *Way to Fly*, and *Against the Common Good* are displayed. As *The Rope is Breaking* appears, a woman's voice cries out, "The rope is breaking!" and the narrator reveals Goya downgraded the figure from the Pope to a priest. The segment concludes with *They Escape Among the Flames*, reinforcing the dark, oppressive atmosphere of Goya's work, which transcends specifics.

Archival Footage.description: Grainy black-and-white footage depicting a chaotic outdoor scene with soldiers in a firing squad execution, reinforcing the historical context of 'The Third of May 1808'.

The Third of May 1808.description: A powerful oil painting by Francisco Goya (1814) showing a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance; shown in full frame with close-ups emphasizing the soldiers and victims. A male speaker notes that this painting clearly distinguished 'the bad guys and the good,' contrasting with the ambiguity of Goya's later etchings.

The Disasters of War (1810-1820).description: A series of black-and-white etchings by Francisco Goya depicting brutal scenes of war, including a man being executed and a woman lying dead, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic contrast. A male narrator explicitly names this as 'The Disasters of War' and specifies it is 'a series of 82 aquatint' that Goya worked on between 1810 and 1819.

Museum Gallery.description: A dimly lit, spacious art gallery with polished floors and white walls, featuring large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series displayed under focused lighting, with visitors observing them in quiet contemplation.

Museum Handlers.description: A group of individuals in casual clothing and white gloves carefully installing a large painting in a museum gallery, emphasizing the artwork’s value and fragility.

Corridor with Paintings.description: A long, arched corridor with dark, framed artworks lining the walls, illuminated by natural light from the far end, creating a solemn, historical atmosphere.

Massacre in Korea Painting.description: A stark, cubist painting by Pablo Picasso (1951) depicting a group of emaciated figures facing a line of soldiers with rifles, rendered in monochromatic tones and angular forms, symbolizing war’s brutality.

Split-Screen Comparison.description: A visual technique used to juxtapose two versions of 'The Third of May 1808'—one with a horse and rider, the other with the firing squad—highlighting the painting’s compositional changes and artistic decisions.

The Execution of Maximillian Painting.description: A painting by Édouard Manet (1868) depicting a firing squad execution, shown in split-screen comparison with 'The Third of May 1808' to highlight thematic continuity.

King Ferdinand VII Painting.description: A formal portrait of King Ferdinand VII in a white military uniform with a gold-embellished sash and high white cravat, set against a dark background, painted in 1817 by Vicente López Portaña, shown in full with the horse and regalia clearly visible. A male narrator refers to him as 'Ferdinand VII' and 'the newly restored king,' explaining his disapproval of certain paintings and his desire to suppress ideas of citizens overthrowing oppressors.

Vietnam II Painting.description: A raw, expressionist painting by Leon Golub (1971) showing soldiers in camouflage with a tank and civilians in distress, rendered in dark, textured brushwork, emphasizing the chaos and violence of war.

What Good is a Cup? Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) portraying a woman offering a cup to a dying man, her face sorrowful, while another figure lies lifeless beside them, rendered in dense, expressive lines, evoking compassion and futility.

Gloomy Presentiments of What Must Come t.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a gaunt, shirtless man with arms outstretched, his face contorted in dread, set against a dark, textured background, evoking despair and foreboding.

Why? Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man being violently attacked by soldiers, his mouth open in a silent scream, rendered with expressive, dark lines, evoking agony and injustice.

Bury Them and Keep Quiet Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man standing over a pile of naked, lifeless bodies, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, evoking silence and complicity.

And it Cannot be Helped Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a woman mourning over a fallen man, her posture slumped in despair, rendered in dense, expressive lines, evoking grief and helplessness.

I Have Seen This Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a woman fleeing with a child as a soldier pursues, with the Spanish inscription 'Yo lo vi' circled in red at the bottom, emphasizing witness and memory. A male speaker highlights this inscription, translating it as 'I have seen this,' connecting it to Goya's personal experience of witnessing atrocities.

The Women are Courageous Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a woman defending a child from an attacker, her expression fierce and determined, rendered in expressive, dark lines.

The Same Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man raising a club over a pile of bodies, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, evoking violence and brutality.

The Rope is Breaking Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man in a robe falling from a rope, surrounded by a crowd of heads, rendered with dramatic shading and expressive lines, symbolizing collapse and chaos. The narration identifies the falling figure as a priest walking on a frayed and tattered rope, and a woman's voice cries out, 'The rope is breaking!' The narrator further explains that Goya, in a rare act of self-censorship, downgraded the figure from the Pope to a priest in this print.

Cartloads for the Cemetery Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting figures loading corpses onto a cart in a dim, shadowed setting, with a woman in a dark dress leaning over the cart, rendered in expressive, dark lines.

They Escape Among the Flames Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing figures fleeing in terror through a fiery, chaotic landscape, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic shading, emphasizing panic and destruction.

Against the Common Good Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a demonic figure with bat wings attacking a man, rendered with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast, evoking supernatural terror.

A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men! Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a horned, monstrous figure dragging a corpse through a rocky landscape, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic shading, evoking horror and absurdity.

The Worst is to Beg Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting emaciated figures huddled around a dead body, one woman in a long dress standing in despair, rendered in expressive, dark lines, emphasizing poverty and suffering. The accompanying narration highlights how an upper-class man cruelly mimics the beggar in this context.

Perhaps They are Another Breed Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing skeletal figures interacting with a man in a hat, suggesting dehumanization and grotesque transformation, rendered with stark, expressive lines.

Way to Fly Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting winged, bat-like creatures flying in darkness, with a central figure with outstretched arms, rendered in stark, expressive lines, evoking surreal horror.

raw_segments: The video opens with grainy black-and-white archival footage of a chaotic outdoor execution, showing a firing squad and distressed civilians, as a male narrator explains that upon Ferdinand VII's return to Madrid in 1814, he "did not approve of the two paintings created in his absence," leading them to be "put in storage" for over 40 years. The scene transitions to a modern museum gallery where a large, dramatic painting is carefully installed by handlers. The camera then moves through a long, arched corridor lined with dark, framed artworks, leading to a close-up of a formal portrait of King Ferdinand VII. The narrator elaborates that "the newly restored king wanted" to avoid reminding citizens of their power to "overthrow their oppressors." The focus shifts to Goya's powerful painting, 'The Third of May 1808', showing a man with raised arms before soldiers, which the narrator identifies as the benchmark against which "all future paintings of violent conflict will be measured," calling it "a powerful statement on tragedy and abuse of power in the name of liberation." A somber, string-based background music begins as a split-screen compares 'The Third of May 1808' with Manet's 'The Execution of Maximilian', followed by Picasso's 'Massacre in Korea' and Golub's 'Vietnam II', visually reinforcing the enduring theme. The narrator continues, stating this message is "as important today as it was 200 years ago." The video then presents Goya’s 'The Disasters of War', a series of etchings depicting brutal scenes, with the narrator providing context that Goya was working on this "series of 82 aquatint" between 1810 and 1819. A final split-screen juxtaposes 'The Third of May 1808' with one of Goya’s etchings, emphasizing the shared themes of violence and resistance across time and media.

The segment opens with a dramatic, full-frame view of Francisco Goya’s *The Third of May 1808*, a powerful oil painting depicting a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark, moonlit sky. A male speaker notes that with this painting, it was "perfectly clear who were the bad guys and who were the good." The camera then transitions to a series of Goya’s etchings from *The Disasters of War*, each shown in close-up with on-screen text identifying the title and publication year (1864). The speaker describes these etchings as "much more ambiguous" and "much more confrontational," even "harder to look at than his black paintings." He explains that Goya "rejects the bombastic heroics of previous war art" and focuses on "the effects on individuals," dispensing with color and using "shadow and shade to show its universal truth," emphasizing, "These are not propaganda, they are reportage." The first etching, *Bury Them and Keep Quiet*, shows a man over lifeless bodies, followed by *The Women are Courageous*, depicting a woman defending a child. Next is *Gloomy Presentiments of What Must Come to Pass*, a haunting image of a gaunt man, then *Why?*, showing a man attacked by soldiers. The speaker continues, explaining that Goya "personally lived through this, seen many of these atrocities with his own eyes," and "tells us so." This is directly linked to the *I Have Seen This* etching, which shows a woman fleeing with a child, featuring the Spanish inscription 'Yo lo vi' circled in red, which the speaker translates as "I have seen this." The sequence concludes with *And it Cannot be Helped*, a woman mourning, and *What Good is a Cup?*, a woman offering a cup to a dying man, as the speaker recounts Goya seeing "people starving to death on the streets" and "executions."

The video rapidly presents a haunting sequence of Francisco Goya’s *The Disasters of War* etchings from 1864, each titled on-screen, depicting grim scenes of human suffering, absurdity, and violence. A narrator explains Goya's bold refusal to take sides, portraying both Spanish and French as cruel and sadistic, a novel approach for its time. The etchings are categorized into three groups: early works on the Napoleonic invasion, followed by those on the famine where Goya witnessed the wealthy mistreating the poor. Visually, *The Same* shows a man with a club over bodies, then *Cartloads for the Cemetery* depicts corpses being loaded. *A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men!* features a monstrous figure dragging a body, while *The Worst is to Beg* shows emaciated figures, with the narrator noting how an upper-class man cruelly mimics the beggar in this context. The sequence continues with skeletal figures in *Perhaps They are Another Breed*, bat-like creatures in *Way to Fly*, and a demonic attack in *Against the Common Good*. The third group of etchings, satirizing the church and Spain's move to absolutism, is introduced as *The Rope is Breaking* appears, visually depicting a man falling from a rope amidst a crowd. A woman's voice cries out, "The rope is breaking!" as the narrator reveals that Goya, in an act of self-censorship, downgraded the figure from the Pope to a priest. The segment concludes with figures fleeing flames in *They Escape Among the Flames*, reinforcing the dark, oppressive atmosphere of Goya's work, which the narrator states transcends specifics.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of Francisco Goya’s dark and expressive etching *Will She Live Again?*, depicting a woman surrounded by shadowy figures, as a narrator highlights Goya's universal compassion for war victims and understanding of human suffering. The scene shifts to *No hay quien los socorra* (Plate 31), an etching showing a lone figure over fallen bodies in a desolate landscape, with the Spanish text circled in red; the narrator emphasizes this image of a desperate survivor crying out, "There is no one to help them." A profile sketch of Goya’s face is shown, followed by a scene from the film *Goya or the Hard Way to the Enlightenment*, set in a dimly lit studio with etchings, where the narrator explains Goya began these works in secret at 62, finishing them under Ferdinand VII, but their publication was delayed 35 years post-mortem due to their incendiary critique of violence and abuse of power. The video returns to Goya’s etchings, including *Not (In This Case) Either* and *What Courage*, visually representing suffering and defiance. A brief shot of a museum corridor with framed paintings and a close-up of a man in a dark coat precedes a formal portrait of Joseph Bonaparte, seated on a golden throne in opulent royal attire, symbolizing the imperial authority and French occupation Goya navigated. The narrator concludes that Ferdinand's censorship and dislike created a difficult period for Goya, leading to a decline in his commissions.

Will She Live Again? Etching.description: A dark, expressive etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a woman lying on the ground, surrounded by shadowy figures, rendered with dense cross-hatching and a sense of despair, which the narrator highlights as embodying Goya's universal compassion for war victims and understanding of suffering.

Corridor with Paintings.description: A long, arched corridor with dark, framed artworks lining the walls, illuminated by natural light from the far end, creating a solemn, historical atmosphere.

Joseph Bonaparte Painting.description: A full-length portrait of Joseph Bonaparte seated on a golden throne, wearing opulent white and gold royal attire with a red cloak, holding a scepter and orb, set against a dark background, symbolizing imperial authority and representing the French occupation period and abuse of power that Goya's work implicitly criticized, as discussed by the narrator.

Goya or the Hard Way to the Enlightenmen.description: A film clip (1971, directed by Konrad Wolf) showing a dimly lit studio with etchings hanging from the ceiling and a man standing in contemplation, illustrating Goya’s artistic process and environment, as the narrator discusses Goya's secret work on his etchings and the political climate he faced.

No hay quien los socorra Etching.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (Plate 31) depicting a lone figure standing over two fallen bodies in a desolate landscape, with the Spanish text 'No hay quien los socorra' circled in red at the bottom, a phrase the narrator quotes and translates as 'There is no one to help them,' emphasizing the scene's depiction of a survivor's desperate cry amidst devastation.

Not (In This Case) Either Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a figure standing over a fallen man, rendered with expressive lines and dramatic shading, evoking despair and helplessness, which the narrator identifies as one of Goya's 'incendiary' works denouncing violence and abuse of power.

What Courage Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a woman standing over a fallen figure near a cannon, her arm raised in defiance, rendered with expressive lines and dramatic contrast, presented by the narrator as another of Goya's 'incendiary' works that challenged authority.

The scene transitions to another etching, *No hay quien los socorra* (Plate 31), where a lone figure stands over fallen bodies in a desolate landscape, with the Spanish text circled in red. The narrator emphasizes this image, stating a lone survivor is faced with devastation and cries out in desperation, "No hay quien lo socorra," which he translates as "There is no one to help them." event_blocks.summary: A sepia-toned photograph of the Quinta del Sordo, Goya’s later residence, shows three figures before its ivy-covered facade, establishing that in 1819, at 73, Goya moved to this farmhouse in the countryside. A top-down scale model of the Quinta del Sordo from 1828 illustrates how Goya, isolated from the Spanish court but still able to see the Royal Palace, created extraordinary, politically charged images while overlooking his former life of privilege. As a dark, unsettling string melody begins, an original 1874 photograph of the painting 'Saturn Devouring His Son' is shown, a dark, unsettling work from the farmhouse, with the camera dramatically zooming on Saturn's monstrous, wild-eyed face as he consumes his child. This grotesque image is jarringly contrasted with an overlay of the formal, brightly lit portrait 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis Painting.' Returning to a close-up of 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' emphasizing its dark, visceral brushwork, the text '8. The Black Paintings (1819-1823)' appears on screen, with the narrator mentioning 'Los Caprichos and the disasters of war' as horrific images Goya withdrew due to fear of reprisal. The Black Paintings, created in isolation from Madrid and safe from prying eyes, allowed Goya to express himself exactly how he wanted. The segment concludes with a detail from 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' depicting dark, contorted figures in a nightmarish scene, reinforcing the oppressive atmosphere of Goya’s late, disturbing works, all set to continuous somber music.

Scale Model of Quinta del Sordo, 1828.description: A miniature, top-down model of the Quinta del Sordo, showing the house and surrounding land in a textured, earthy landscape.

8. The Black Paintings (1819-1823).description: Text overlay identifying the period and series of Goya’s late, dark works, appearing over a close-up of 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' explicitly mentioned by the narrator as a series where Goya, isolated from Madrid, could express himself exactly how he wanted, safe from prying eyes.

The Family of the Infante Don Luis Paint.description: A formal, brightly lit portrait of an aristocratic family in 18th-century attire, featuring rich colors and elegant poses, shown in a layered overlay with the Black Paintings.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, and referred to as part of 'The Black Paintings' series.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya, showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black, identified as one of the 'horrific images' and part of 'The Black Paintings' series where Goya could express himself freely.

Original Photo from the Farmhouse.description: A black-and-white photograph from 1874 showing a dark, damaged painting of a monstrous figure, identified as 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' with visible frame and archival markings.

Quinta del Sordo (House of the Deaf).description: A historical building with an arched doorway, ivy-covered walls, and three figures in 19th-century attire standing before it, shown in a sepia-toned photograph, which the narrator identifies as 'a farmhouse in the countryside' where Goya moved at 73 and from which he could see the Royal Palace.

This transitions to a top-down scale model of the Quinta del Sordo from 1828, while the narrator details how Goya, isolated from the Spanish court, created extraordinary images, even though he could still see the Royal Palace from his house, allowing him to create politically charged works while overlooking his former life of privilege. As the narrator states Goya was 'free to create monsters,' a dark, unsettling string melody begins, accompanying an original 1874 photograph of 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' a dark, unsettling painting from the farmhouse. The camera then dramatically zooms on Saturn's monstrous, wild-eyed face as he consumes his child. This grotesque image is jarringly contrasted with an overlay of the formal, brightly lit portrait 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis Painting.' The scene returns to a close-up of 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' emphasizing its dark, visceral brushwork, with the text '8. The Black Paintings (1819-1823)' appearing on screen. The segment concludes with a detail from 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' depicting dark, contorted figures in a nightmarish scene, reinforcing the oppressive atmosphere of Goya’s late, disturbing works, all set to the continuous somber music.

event_blocks.summary: A digital reconstruction of Quinta del Sordo, dimly lit and sepia-toned, reveals Goya's 14 Black Paintings, including 'Two Old Men Eating Soup' and 'Witches’ Sabbath', displayed on its walls, with a 3D wireframe model illustrating their spatial arrangement as a narrator explains Goya created these provocative works directly onto the wallpaper, unseen and untiled by anyone but his maid. Archival photographs from 1874, showing damaged versions of 'Saturn Devouring His Son' and 'Witches’ Sabbath', reinforce that Goya intended them as private frescoes, not public displays. A clip from 'Goya or the Hard Way to Enlightenment' precedes a title card, '9. Removing the Paintings', accompanied by melancholy acoustic guitar music, as Baron Émile d’Erlanger, who purchased the house after Goya's death, is shown. Grainy archival footage then depicts the meticulous 'Strappo' technique, where two men peel fragile paint layers from the walls onto canvas, a delicate conservation process initiated by d’Erlanger. Earlier archival footage shows 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Réunion de sorciers' in damaged frames, with a narrator noting repairs and alterations. An architectural drawing details the spatial arrangement of Black Paintings, including 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', within a reconstructed room, noting their 1874 in situ photography. In a modern museum gallery, visitors observe 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting' under focused lighting, with the narrator emphasizing the importance of these records. The 'Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painting' is shown, with the narrator questioning the overemphasis on Goya's deafness and madness, citing his 30 years of deafness and lack of evidence for madness in his letters. A sepia-toned clip from *Napoleon* depicts a chaotic crowd, as the narrator urges viewing the paintings within their political and religious context, shifting to dramatic orchestral music. A sequence of Black Paintings, including 'Two Men Fighting', 'A Man with a Winged Monster', and 'The Women Laughing Painting', are presented as the works of an "old, tired man, stripped of his status and wealth, disappointed and angry with the world, and desperate to express it in the only way he knows how. In paint." The segment concludes with a dense grid montage of multiple Black Paintings, such as 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', 'Altropos (The Fates) Painting', 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis Painting', 'Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting', and 'Majo With Guitar (Detail) Painting'.

Archival Footage of Strappo Technique.description: Grainy black-and-white archival footage showing two men in white coats and dark suits meticulously removing paint from a wall using tools, with visible cracks and peeling paint, highlighting the physical effort and precision required in the conservation process. The narrator describes this as the process of peeling the paintings from the walls and transferring them onto canvas, comparing the delicate task to stripping wallpaper.

Baron Émile d’Erlanger.description: A black-and-white portrait of a man with a mustache, wearing a dark suit and bow tie, identified as Baron Émile d’Erlanger, a key figure in the preservation of Goya’s Black Paintings. The narrator explicitly states that he bought the house in 1863, long after Goya's death, and was responsible for having Goya's paintings peeled from the walls along with the wallpaper and transferred onto canvas.

Reconstruction of Quinta del Sordo.description: A dimly lit, sepia-toned digital reconstruction of the interior of the Quinta del Sordo, featuring large, dark paintings on the walls, including the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting' and the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting', with a central doorway and a transparent 3D wireframe model overlaying the space to illustrate the spatial arrangement of the artworks. The narrator describes this as the setting where Goya produced 14 extraordinary, provocative works directly onto the wallpaper of his house's two floors, living like a hermit and never mentioning, signing, or titling them, with only his maid ever seeing them.

Strappo Technique.description: Grainy black-and-white archival footage showing two men in white coats and dark suits carefully peeling the paint layers from the walls of the Quinta del Sordo using tools, illustrating the delicate and labor-intensive process of removing the Black Paintings using the 'Strappo' technique.

Goya or the Hard Way to Enlightenment.description: A film clip (1971, directed by Konrad Wolf) showing a man in a white coat observing a large, dark painting, likely the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting', in a dimly lit studio, with candles providing ambient light, illustrating Goya’s artistic environment.

Original Photo from the Farmhouse.description: A black-and-white archival photograph from 1874 showing a damaged version of the 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting' and the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting', with visible frame and archival markings, emphasizing their deteriorated condition and historical context. The narrator's voice reinforces that Goya created these as fresco paintings, never expecting them to be displayed publicly.

The Women Laughing Painting.description: A dark, unsettling painting by Francisco Goya depicting two women with wide, distorted grins, their features blurred and eerie, evoking a sense of madness or mockery, shown in a close-up, presented as part of a series of Black Paintings that the narrator describes as the expression of 'an old, tired man, stripped of his status and wealth, disappointed and angry with the world'.

Two Men Fighting Painting.description: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya showing two men in a violent struggle with sticks, set against a rocky landscape, rendered in rough, dynamic brushwork, shown in full frame, presented as part of a series of Black Paintings that the narrator describes as the expression of 'an old, tired man, stripped of his status and wealth, disappointed and angry with the world'.

Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting.description: A somber, dark painting by Francisco Goya showing two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair, rendered in rough, expressive strokes, shown in both the architectural drawing and the modern museum gallery.

Réunion de sorciers Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, shown in archival black-and-white footage with visible frame damage and museum labels, which the narrator explains had 'panels lost, and sections had to be repaired by restorers, and some were purposely altered for aesthetic reasons'.

Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painti.description: A somber self-portrait of Goya appearing frail and ill, supported by Doctor Arrieta who holds a glass, painted in 1820, shown in a close-up with dark, expressive brushwork and a dark background, which the narrator uses to discuss Goya's deafness and refute claims of madness, stating Goya 'had been deaf for over 30 years at this point, and there is no evidence of madness in his letters or elsewhere'.

A Man with a Winged Monster Painting.description: An oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a man being attacked by a winged, demonic figure, rendered in dark, expressive brushwork, shown in a close-up with dramatic shading, presented as part of a series of Black Paintings that the narrator describes as the expression of 'an old, tired man, stripped of his status and wealth, disappointed and angry with the world'.

Altropos (The Fates) Painting.description: A dark, haunting oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819–1823) depicting three grotesque, dark figures in a desolate landscape, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork, evoking themes of fate and decay, shown in the final grid montage, and mentioned by the narrator as one of '14 of them in total'.

Majo With Guitar (Detail) Painting.description: A close-up detail of a man in a tricorn hat with a contemplative, upward gaze, from Francisco Goya’s 1779 painting, showcasing his early, more naturalistic and expressive style, shown in the final grid montage, and mentioned by the narrator as one of '14 of them in total'.

Museum Gallery.description: A dimly lit, spacious art gallery with polished floors and white walls, featuring large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series displayed under focused lighting, with visitors observing them in quiet contemplation, shown in a wide shot.

Napoleon Painting.description: A black-and-white triptych from Abel Gance’s 1927 film *Napoléon*, showing Napoleon on horseback flanked by soldiers in a panoramic, dynamic composition, emphasizing historical grandeur and cinematic innovation, shown in a sepia-toned clip, used by the narrator to contextualize Goya's works within 'political and religious turmoil', accompanied by dramatic, urgent orchestral music.

The Family of the Infante Don Luis Paint.description: A formal, brightly lit portrait of an aristocratic family in 18th-century attire, featuring rich colors and elegant poses, shown in a layered overlay with the Black Paintings in the final grid montage, and mentioned by the narrator as one of '14 of them in total'.

Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting.description: A portrait of Francisco Goya (1790) in a red dress and black hat, holding a paintbrush and palette, standing before an easel, representing his earlier, more confident and vibrant artistic phase, shown in the final grid montage, and mentioned by the narrator as one of '14 of them in total'.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, shown in archival black-and-white footage with visible frame damage and museum labels, which the narrator explains had 'panels lost, and sections had to be repaired by restorers, and some were purposely altered for aesthetic reasons'.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya, showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black, shown in the final grid montage, and mentioned by the narrator as one of '14 of them in total'.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dimly lit, sepia-toned digital reconstruction of the Quinta del Sordo, showcasing large, dark paintings like 'Two Old Men Eating Soup' and 'Witches’ Sabbath' on its walls, as a narrator explains Goya produced an extraordinary cycle of 14 works painted directly onto the wallpaper of his house's two floors. A transparent 3D wireframe model overlays the room, gradually filling with images of Goya’s Black Paintings, illustrating their spatial arrangement, while the narrator emphasizes Goya lived like a hermit, never mentioning, signing, or titling these provocative works, which only his maid saw. The scene transitions to archival black-and-white photographs from 1874, labeled 'Original Photo from the Farmhouse', showing damaged versions of 'Saturn Devouring His Son' and 'Witches’ Sabbath', reinforcing the narrator's point that Goya created them as fresco paintings, never expecting them to be displayed publicly. A clip from the 1971 film 'Goya or the Hard Way to Enlightenment' then shows a man observing a large painting in a dimly lit studio, before a title card '9. Removing the Paintings' appears, accompanied by melancholy acoustic guitar music. A portrait of Baron Émile d’Erlanger is shown as the narrator reveals he bought the house in 1863, long after Goya's death. The segment concludes with grainy black-and-white archival footage of the 'Strappo' technique, where two men meticulously peel the fragile paint layers from the walls, illustrating the delicate and labor-intensive conservation process that Baron d’Erlanger initiated to transfer the paintings from the walls onto canvas, a task the narrator likens to the impossible delicacy of stripping wallpaper.

The video begins with grainy, black-and-white archival footage of Goya's 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Réunion de sorciers', displayed in their original damaged frames with visible archival labels, as a male narrator explains that "panels were lost, and sections had to be repaired by restorers, and some were purposely altered for aesthetic reasons." The scene transitions to a detailed architectural drawing of a gallery layout, showing the spatial arrangement of Goya’s Black Paintings, including the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', within a reconstructed room, while the narrator notes that "Luckily for posterity, the paintings were photographed in situ in 1874, before they were removed." This shifts to a modern, dimly lit museum gallery, labeled '10. Context', where visitors observe large, dark Black Paintings, including the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', under focused lighting, as the narrator emphasizes that "thanks to these images, we have a record of where they were, and what condition they were in," accompanied by a soft, melancholic acoustic guitar melody. A close-up of the 'Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painting', depicting a frail Goya supported by Doctor Arrieta, is shown as the narrator states, "There's more than one way to look at these paintings. I think the deafness, madness angle has been overstated," continuing to assert that Goya "had been deaf for over 30 years at this point, and there is no evidence of madness in his letters or elsewhere." The video then cuts to a sepia-toned film clip from Abel Gance’s 1927 *Napoleon*, depicting a chaotic crowd scene, as the narrator urges, "We have to see them in the context of political and religious turmoil," with the background music shifting to a dramatic, urgent orchestral score. This is followed by a series of Goya’s Black Paintings: 'Two Men Fighting', 'A Man with a Winged Monster', and 'The Women Laughing Painting', while the narrator describes them as works "by an old, tired man, stripped of his status and wealth, disappointed and angry with the world, and desperate to express it in the only way he knows how. In paint." The sequence culminates in a grid montage of multiple Black Paintings, including 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', 'Altropos (The Fates) Painting', and 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis Painting', layered with the 'Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting' and 'Majo With Guitar (Detail) Painting', creating a dense, overlapping visual narrative, as the narrator states, "There are 14 of them in total..."

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a grid montage of Francisco Goya’s "Black Paintings," including 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting,' 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' and 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting,' with a narrator explaining Goya's technique of using a thick black paint base on plaster, then building characters with lighter shades and a limited oil palette, employing broad, energetic brushwork that echoed a morally chaotic world. Close-ups highlight the emaciated figures in 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting' and the grotesque grins in 'Women Laughing Painting.' The scene shifts to a museum gallery where visitors examine 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting,' identified as a later, disillusioned depiction of the same subject Goya painted 30 years prior, contrasting sharply with the vibrant, sunlit 'The Meadow of San Isidro' (1788), a joyful festival scene. The narrative then delves into historical context, explaining that 30 years after the light-filled 'The Meadow of San Isidro,' Spain experienced the French Revolution, Napoleon's rise and fall, the occupation of Spain, a guerrilla war, the Inquisition, and the restoration of a dictator king, a world that had become a nightmare. This is visually represented by a formal portrait of Napoleon Bonaparte, followed by chaotic battle and fiery ceremonial scenes. The earlier 'The Meadow of San Isidro,' depicting a celebration with young people dancing and drinking, is contrasted with the dark, grim 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting,' where the sky turns stormy, the music becomes dissonant, and the source of music is a lone madman, with the crowd howling in agony. The narrator notes that the reason for their pain is Napoleon Bonaparte, positioned in the middle of the suffering mass. The video further explores Goya's 'Black Paintings,' refuting the idea they lacked meaning, and presents 'Burial of the Sardine,' a chaotic celebration with grotesque masks, and 'A Procession of the Flagellants,' depicting bloodied figures whipping themselves, which Spanish liberals like Goya saw as representing ignorance and superstition. Returning to 'Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro,' the focus is on grim-faced women and a prominent man in 17th-century attire, emphasizing Goya's portrayal of dark, oppressive themes.

Museum Gallery.description: A dimly lit, spacious art gallery with polished floors and white walls, featuring large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series displayed under focused lighting, with visitors observing them in quiet contemplation, shown in a wide shot.

The Meadow of San Isidro.description: A panoramic, brightly lit oil painting by Francisco Goya (1788) depicting a festive gathering in a meadow with a view of Madrid in the background, featuring figures in light-colored clothing, parasols, and a lively crowd. The narrator describes it as 'a celebration, full of young people dancing and drinking and having fun,' and it is accompanied by a lighter, festive, brass band-like musical tune.

Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting.description: A somber, dark painting by Francisco Goya showing two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair, rendered in rough, expressive strokes, shown in both the architectural drawing and the modern museum gallery.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, shown in archival black-and-white footage with visible frame damage and museum labels.

A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting.description: A crowded, frenzied scene by Francisco Goya, identified by the speaker as 'another procession to the sanctuary of San Isidro' and also known as 'the Inquisition.' It depicts figures in ecstatic or frenzied states with contorted faces, rendered in dark, expressive brushwork. The video focuses on a group of women in dark shawls and head coverings, one holding a small object, with a white outline highlighting a specific woman in the foreground. The speaker notes Goya dressed the figures in '17th-century fashions' to represent 'ignorance and superstition,' and describes a prominent man in the painting dressed in 17th-century clothes with a mustache and goatee, holding a sword and a roll of paper.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya, showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black, shown in the final grid montage.

Women Laughing Painting.description: A dark, unsettling painting by Francisco Goya depicting two women with wide, distorted grins, their features blurred and eerie, evoking a sense of madness or mockery.

Napoleon Painting.description: A black-and-white triptych from Abel Gance’s 1927 film *Napoléon*, showing Napoleon on horseback flanked by soldiers in a panoramic, dynamic composition, emphasizing historical grandeur and cinematic innovation, shown in a sepia-toned clip.

A Procession of the Flagellants Painting.description: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya (c. 1812-1819) depicting bloodied, conical-hatted figures whipping themselves in a public ritual, surrounded by a dense, watching crowd, with on-screen text identifying the title and artist. The speaker refers to it as 'the dark and cruel Procession of the Flagellants' when discussing Goya's various procession depictions.

Burial of the Sardine Painting.description: A chaotic, celebratory oil painting by Francisco Goya (c. 1812-1819) showing a crowd of figures in white dresses dancing wildly, some with grotesque masks, under a dark, turbulent sky, with on-screen text identifying the title and artist. The speaker refers to it as 'the lighter-toned Burial of the Sardine' when discussing Goya's various procession depictions.

raw_segments: The video opens with a grid montage of Francisco Goya’s dark, expressive paintings, including the visceral 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting', and the somber 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', as a male narrator explains that these works are linked technically, with Goya using a thick layer of black paint directly onto plaster walls as a base, then creating characters with lighter shades of whites, grays, ochres, and earth. The oil palette is described as very limited, with Goya's brushwork being broad, energetic, and loose, echoing a morally chaotic world, and impressionistic long before the Impressionists existed. The sequence transitions to a close-up of the emaciated figures in the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', followed by a detailed zoom into the grotesque, distorted grins of the two women in the 'Women Laughing Painting'. The scene then shifts to a museum gallery where visitors gather around a large, dark painting, identified by on-screen text as '11. Pilgrimage to San Isadora', as the narrator invites viewers to look at the theories behind the 'Black Paintings' one by one, accompanied by melancholic acoustic guitar music. The camera focuses on the frenzied crowd and contorted faces of 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting', which the narrator identifies as showing the passage of Goya's disillusionment and being the exact same subject matter painted by Goya 30 years previously. This is followed by a contrasting cut to 'The Meadow of San Isidro' by Goya (1788), a vibrant, sunlit festival scene with a panoramic view of Madrid, labeled with on-screen text 'The Meadow of San Isidro, Francisco Goya, 1788'. The narrator explains that this earlier work, painted in 1788, depicts the feast of Saint Isidora and is full of light and joy, before the video concludes with a return to the dark 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting', reinforcing the stark thematic shift in Goya’s vision.

The segment opens with a historical reenactment or film clip depicting a bustling colonial-era scene, featuring soldiers in blue and white uniforms, horses, and a crowd around a wooden platform, all set to an ominous, low background music. A narrator explains that "30 years later, that world had become a nightmare," detailing "30 years, which saw the French Revolution, the rise and fall of Napoleon, the occupation of Spain, and the horrendous guerrilla war that followed," along with "the extension of the Inquisition, and the restoration of a dictator king." Visually, this transitions to a formal portrait of Napoleon Bonaparte, seated in a white military uniform with a green overcoat, exuding authority and contemplation. The scene then shifts to a chaotic battle painting with mounted warriors, followed by a dramatic, fiery religious or ceremonial event with figures in ornate robes and flames, all while the intense background music continues. The narrative then moves to a panoramic, light-filled painting, 'The Meadow of San Isidro,' depicting a festive gathering with elegantly dressed figures relaxing on a hillside overlooking Madrid. The narrator describes this as "a celebration, full of young people dancing and drinking and having fun," accompanied by a lighter, more festive, brass band-like tune. This is starkly contrasted with 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting,' a dark, grim depiction of a crowd in distress, showing figures in anguish and despair. The narrator explains, "The later version is the exact same scene, but the sky has turned from glorious sunshine to dark and stormy," as the music becomes dissonant, darker, and unsettling. The two paintings are shown side-by-side, emphasizing the contrast. Close-ups of the dark painting highlight the contorted, agonized faces of the crowd, while the narrator adds, "Where once the revelers sang along to brass bands, the source of the music is now a lone madman," and "The once orderly crowd are now a seething mass of madness, no longer laughing, but howling in agony," with the unsettling music intensifying into a distinct, wailing sound. Finally, a split-screen juxtaposes the formal portrait of Napoleon with the dark, suffering crowd, drawing a thematic connection between imperial power and human suffering. The narrator concludes, "And the reason for their pain is right in the middle of the mass of bodies, staring out at us," before naming "Napoleon, Bonaparte," as the wailing sound peaks and then fades slightly, reinforcing the video’s exploration of historical violence and social contrast through close-ups of the distressed faces.

The segment opens with a close-up of Goya's 'Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro,' a dark, expressive painting depicting a frenzied crowd with contorted, screaming faces, rendered in thick, chaotic brushwork. A male speaker begins by discussing the identification of Goya's black paintings, contradicting the belief that they lacked real meaning or were merely products of Goya's psychiatric decline. As the camera slowly zooms out to reveal the full composition of the 'Pilgrimage,' showing a long procession under a stormy sky, solemn, traditional Spanish string music begins to play. The speaker identifies this painting as 'another procession to the sanctuary of San Isidro,' noting its proximity to Goya's farmhouse, and adds that it's also known as 'the Inquisition.' The scene then transitions to 'Burial of the Sardine,' a chaotic, celebratory crowd with figures in white dresses and grotesque masks under a dark sky, which the speaker refers to as 'the lighter-toned Burial of the Sardine.' This is followed by 'A Procession of the Flagellants,' showing bloodied, conical-hatted figures whipping themselves, which the speaker calls 'the dark and cruel Procession of the Flagellants.' The speaker explains that for Spanish liberals like Goya, these ceremonies represented 'ignorance and superstition,' something from the past. The video returns to the 'Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro,' focusing on a group of grim-faced women in dark shawls, one holding a small object, with a white outline highlighting a specific woman. The speaker emphasizes Goya's portrayal of these themes by dressing the figures in '17th-century fashions' and describes a prominent man in the painting, dressed in 17th-century clothes with a mustache and goatee, holding a sword and a roll of paper. The overall atmosphere remains dark, oppressive, and emotionally charged, reflecting Goya’s signature style.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of a dark, dramatic religious painting, depicting an inquisitor in a Spanish 'gollia' collar and necklace holding a cross over a praying woman in yellow and a man in dark clothing, with the speaker identifying the inquisitor as colluding with a friar and the surrounding figures as 'Celestinas or procuresses' who exploit young girls. The camera then transitions to the 'Maja and Celestine on a Balcony' painting, showing a young woman in gold with an older man, maintaining a dimly lit atmosphere Goya used to emphasize 'superstitious beliefs' by shrouding characters in darkness against distant light. This somber mood persists through a wide shot of 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro,' featuring a long procession under a stormy sky, and a close-up of women with fearful or ecstatic expressions. Somber string music accompanies the cut to the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath' painting, which displays a demonic figure amidst grotesque forms. A speaker elaborates on restoration alterations to this painting, noting the original was 'much wider' before a restorer removed canvas sides, disrupting Goya's use of empty space and shifting the 'center of balance' by moving a 'woman in white' closer to the middle, a change visually demonstrated through split-screen and grid comparisons with archival black-and-white versions showing damage and fading alongside the restored color versions.

Maja and Celestine on a Balcony Painting.description: A detailed painting by Francisco Goya (1812) showing a young woman in a golden dress with a white shawl, her arms crossed, standing beside an older man who is adjusting her necklace. The painting's title, featuring 'Celestine,' directly links it to the speaker's discussion of Goya's portrayal of 'Celestinas or procuresses' as characters, often 'old women who exploit young girls,' suggesting a thematic connection to exploitation.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure with horns surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene, characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere. The original work was significantly wider, but a restorer later removed the sides of the canvas, altering Goya's intended use of empty space for dramatic effect and shifting the painting's center of balance by positioning a woman in white nearer the middle. This alteration and the painting's conservation history are visually demonstrated through archival black-and-white footage showing damage and fading, contrasted with current, restored color versions.

A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting.description: A crowded, frenzied scene by Francisco Goya showing a religious procession with figures in ecstatic or frenzied states, their faces contorted, rendered in dark, expressive brushwork; the video focuses on a group of women in dark shawls and head coverings, one holding a small object, with a white outline highlighting a specific woman in the foreground.

Réunion de sorciers Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, shown in archival black-and-white footage with visible frame damage and museum labels.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a dark, dramatic religious painting, where a robed figure, identified by a speaker as an 'inquisitor' in a uniquely Spanish 'gollia' collar and necklace, holds a cross over two seated individuals: a praying woman in yellow and a man in dark clothing. The speaker notes the inquisitor is colluding with a 'friar' next to him, and the surrounding characters are 'Celestinas or procuresses,' whom Goya often portrayed as 'old women who exploit young girls.' This thematic context is visually reinforced as the camera shifts to 'Maja and Celestine on a Balcony,' depicting a young woman in a golden dress with an older man. The somber, dimly lit atmosphere, which the speaker explains Goya used to emphasize 'superstitious beliefs' by shrouding main characters in darkness against a distant light, continues through a wide shot of 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro,' showing a long procession of figures against a stormy sky, and a close-up of women with fearful or ecstatic expressions. A somber, string-based background music begins as the video cuts to the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath' painting, featuring a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing forms. A speaker highlights this work as an example of restoration alterations, explaining that the original 'Witches’ Sabbath' was 'much wider' before a restorer removed the canvas sides, ignoring Goya's use of empty space for dramatic effect and changing the 'center of balance' by positioning a 'woman in white' nearer the middle. This alteration is visually demonstrated through a split-screen and grid comparison of the painting in various states, revealing archival black-and-white versions with visible damage, fading, and frame issues alongside the current, restored color versions.

event_blocks.summary: The 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' is introduced with a close-up on its central demonic figure, identified as 'the he-goat or Satan,' a dark, horned, goat-like creature with a single eye, depicted preaching from a mound with its mouth open, instructing the surrounding 'old crones' and 'obsequious women with bestial twisted faces, thought to be witches'; this figure, dressed as a priest, makes an anti-clerical statement, as the camera pans right revealing more grotesque, writhing human figures in the nightmarish scene. The narrative shifts to Goya's 'Witches’ Flight' (1797), showing figures flailing in mid-air against a black background, followed by his etching 'Gone for Good' (1799), which depicts a flying woman with the inscription 'Volaverunt.' Returning to a full view of the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' the horned figure, adorned with a leafy crown, is seen amidst monstrous figures in a desolate landscape, while the speaker contextualizes Goya's use of witchcraft imagery within Spain's historical blend of Enlightenment, Catholicism, and supernatural belief. The scene abruptly changes to José de Ribera’s 'Ixion' (1632), a dark, tenebrist painting, with 'Tenebrism' overlaid, illustrating the dramatic chiaroscuro that Goya employed, a technique popularized by 17th-century Spanish artists like Ribera, whom Goya admired. The final shot is a close-up of Rembrandt’s 'Lucretia' (1666), a somber portrait of a woman in a white and gold dress holding a dagger, her face illuminated against a dark background, presented as an example of Rembrandt, Goya's hero, also using tenebrism to heighten dramatic effect.

Lucretia Painting.description: A somber, tenebrist oil painting by Rembrandt (1666) showing a woman in a white and gold dress, holding a dagger, her face illuminated against a dark background, with text identifying the title and artist, and presented as an example of Rembrandt's use of tenebrism to heighten dramatic effect.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure, identified by the speaker as 'the he-goat or Satan,' with horns and a leafy crown, dressed as a priest and preaching from a small mound with his mouth wide open, giving instructions to surrounding 'old crones' and 'obsequious women with bestial twisted faces, thought to be witches,' all within a nightmarish scene characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere in a desolate landscape.

Witches’ Flight Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya (1797) showing figures in mid-air against a black background, their limbs flailing in a chaotic, almost falling motion, with text identifying the title and artist.

Gone for Good Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) depicting a woman in a flowing black dress flying above a group of men, with the Latin inscription 'Volaverunt' at the bottom.

Ixion Painting.description: A dark, tenebrist oil painting by José de Ribera (1632) showing a muscular figure in torment, with dramatic chiaroscuro and deep shadows, visually highlighted with the word 'Tenebrism' overlaid, and noted by the speaker as a work by an artist who popularized tenebrism and was admired by Goya.

This dark, horned, goat-like creature, with a single visible eye, is described as preaching from a small mound with its mouth wide open, giving instructions to the 'old crones' and 'obsequious women with bestial twisted faces, thought to be witches' that surround it. The speaker notes the figure is dressed as a priest, making an anti-clerical statement. The camera slowly pans right, revealing more of the chaotic, nightmarish scene with grotesque, writhing human figures. The scene transitions to Goya's 'Witches’ Flight' (1797), showing figures flailing in mid-air against a black background, followed by his black-and-white etching 'Gone for Good' (1799), depicting a flying woman with the Latin inscription 'Volaverunt.' The video then returns to a full view of the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' showing the horned figure adorned with a leafy crown amidst a crowd of monstrous figures in a desolate landscape. The scene abruptly changes to José de Ribera’s dark, tenebrist painting 'Ixion' (1632), with the word 'Tenebrism' overlaid, highlighting the dramatic chiaroscuro.
