event_blocks.summary: The Witches’ Sabbath Painting, a nightmarish composition featuring a horned, black-robed he-goat figure and a throng of monstrous forms, is presented as a critique of the church's condemnation of science and enlightened thinkers. This is followed by Goya's etchings: 'There Was No Help,' depicting a man with a staff amidst distorted faces, and 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters,' showing a sleeping man with nocturnal creatures. The Two Old Men Painting, characterized by emaciated, skeletal faces and intense blackness, is then introduced with somber music, where one man holds a cane and a beast-like figure whispers into his ear. The etching 'Tale-Bearers' shows a winged demon over a sleeping man, linking to Goya's theme of irrationality. The video then focuses on a dark, expressive oil painting, possibly a self-portrait of Goya as a gaunt, bearded figure with a staff, with a smaller demonic figure shouting at him, potentially alluding to Goya's deafness. A comparison is made to a monochrome sketch, and the painting's various titles, like 'the two monks,' are discussed. A brief, contrasting pastoral scene, 'The Picnic' Painting, appears before returning to the dark painting, with the narrator referencing Goya's influences: nature, Rembrandt, and Velázquez. This leads to a comparison of Goya's portrait of Francisco Cabarrús with Velázquez's portrait of Pablo de Valladolid, noting the former is based on the latter. The video concludes by returning to the dark painting, shown in a mirrored split-screen, emphasizing its haunting composition and the unsettling demonic presence.

Tale-Bearers Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) showing a winged demon hovering over a sleeping man, with the word 'Soplones' inscribed below, rendered with expressive lines and dramatic shading.

Two Old Men Painting.description: A somber oil painting of two emaciated, skeletal figures, identified as a Black Painting by Francisco Goya. It depicts a tall, gaunt figure with a long white beard and dark robes, holding a staff, suggested by the narrator to be a self-portrait of Goya in his 70s, with a smaller, demonic-looking figure clinging to his shoulder, mouth agape in a silent scream, described by the narrator as 'shouting' and possibly alluding to Goya's deafness. Rendered in thick, textured brushwork with deep browns and ochres, evoking horror and decay, it is also known as 'the two monks' due to the bearded man's possible monk's habit. The painting is shown in close-up, full view, in comparison with a preparatory sketch, and later with a mirrored version of itself.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure with horns adorned with a leafy crown, surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene, characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere. The painting, which the narrator describes as 'dangerous' due to its context during the Inquisition, features a central cloaked, ghostly white figure, interpreted as a new initiate or a nun, among the 'he-goat and his followers,' who are presented as the 'antithesis of reason and enlightened thinking,' serving as a 'bitter critique of a church' that condemned science and accused enlightened thinkers of being 'agents of the devil.'

The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters Et.description: A black-and-white etching of a sleeping man surrounded by owls and bats, symbolizing the dangers of ignorance, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' visible on a block beside him, rendered in dense cross-hatching and dramatic contrast.

There Was No Help Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) showing a man in a tall, striped hat holding a staff, surrounded by a crowd of distorted, grotesque faces, rendered with expressive lines and dramatic contrast.

Pablo de Valladolid Painting.description: A full-length portrait by Diego Velázquez (1635) of Pablo de Valladolid, shown in a dark, puffed-sleeve doublet with a white ruff collar, blue hose, and black shoes, standing with one hand extended and the other on his hip, set against a plain, light-brown background, mirroring the pose of the Cabarrús portrait. The narrator notes that Goya's portrait of Francisco Cabarrús was based on this work, highlighting Velázquez's influence on Goya.

The Picnic Painting.description: A bright, pastoral oil painting from 1776 showing a lively gathering of figures under trees, with a woman in a red dress holding a basket, a man in a black hat, and others reclining on the grass, set against a landscape with hills and a pale sky, contrasting sharply with the preceding dark works.

Francisco Cabarrús Painting.description: A full-length portrait by Francisco Goya (1788) of Francisco Cabarrús, depicted in a light green coat with silver trim, white breeches, white stockings, and black shoes, standing with one hand extended and the other on his hip, set against a dark, neutral background. The narrator explicitly states this portrait by Goya 'is said to have been based on the Velázquez portrait of Pablo de Valladolid,' reflecting Goya's admiration for Velázquez.

raw_segments: The video begins with a close-up of the central figure in the Witches’ Sabbath Painting—a cloaked, ghostly white figure, which the narrator suggests looks "suspiciously like a nun" and a "new initiate into the coven," seated among a chaotic, dark crowd of grotesque, demonic figures. The camera slowly zooms out, revealing the full, nightmarish composition of the painting, with a horned, black-robed figure on the left, identified by the narrator as the "he-goat" whose "followers are the very antithesis of reason and enlightened thinking," and a dense throng of writhing, monstrous forms. The narrator describes this "dangerous painting" as a "bitter critique of a church" that condemned science and accused enlightened thinkers of being "agents of the devil," especially given the Inquisition was still active. The scene transitions to a series of etchings from Goya’s Los Caprichos: first, 'There Was No Help,' showing a man in a tall hat holding a staff, surrounded by a crowd of distorted faces; then, 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters,' depicting a sleeping man with owls, bats, and a cat, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' visible. The video returns to the Witches’ Sabbath Painting, now shown in full frame, emphasizing its dark, oppressive atmosphere. A title card appears: '14. Two Old Men,' accompanied by the start of somber, melancholic string music, followed by a close-up of the Two Old Men Painting, focusing on the emaciated, skeletal faces of the two figures, one with a long beard and the other with a grimacing expression, both rendered in thick, expressive brushwork. The narrator highlights how the "intense blackness" enveloping them "emphasizes the characters," noting that "one man is holding a cane, and another beast-like figure appears to be talking into his ear." The video then cuts to the etching 'Tale-Bearers,' showing a winged demon hovering over a sleeping man, with the word 'Soplones' inscribed below, which the narrator connects to Goya's common theme of "insanity or irrationality represented by demons hovering over men." The final shot returns to the Two Old Men Painting, with a close-up on the two faces, highlighting their hollow eyes and contorted features, conveying profound despair and decay, as the narrator mentions art scholars pointing out that "the round-shaped mouth in Goya's art often indicates..." before the audio cuts off.

The video opens with a close-up of a dark, expressive oil painting, identified as a Black Painting by Francisco Goya, depicting two grotesque, bearded figures in a nightmarish embrace, rendered in thick, textured brushwork with deep browns and ochres, evoking horror and decay. A narrator immediately highlights a 'demon figure's communication,' explaining that the smaller, demonic-looking figure clinging to the shoulder of the tall, gaunt figure is 'shouting,' suggesting this could be an allusion to Goya's deafness. As the camera slowly zooms out to reveal the full composition, the narrator posits that the tall figure with a long white beard and dark robes, holding a staff, is a 'self-portrait of Goya,' who was well into his 70s. The scene transitions to a split-screen comparison, with the full painting on the left and a monochrome sketch of a similar bearded figure with a staff on the right, suggesting a preparatory study. The narrator mentions that Goya didn't name his Black Paintings, leading to various titles over the years, including 'the two monks' for this work, due to the bearded man possibly wearing a monk's habit. The video then cuts to a full-screen view of the dark painting, emphasizing its oppressive atmosphere, before a brief, unrelated shot of a bright, pastoral scene, 'The Picnic' by Goya (1776), appears. Returning to the dark painting, the narrator recalls Goya's statement that his teachers were 'nature, Rembrandt and Velázquez,' emphasizing Goya's worship of Velázquez. This leads into a split-screen comparison of two full-length portraits: on the left, Francisco Goya’s 1788 portrait of Francisco Cabarrús in a green coat and white breeches, and on the right, Diego Velázquez’s 1635 portrait of Pablo de Valladolid in a dark, puffed-sleeve doublet, both standing in similar poses. The narrator confirms that Goya's portrait of Francisco Cabarrús 'is said to have been based on the Velázquez portrait of Pablo de Valladolid.' The video concludes with a return to the dark painting, now shown in a split-screen with a slightly different, mirrored version of itself, as the narrator suggests comparing it to a flipped version, reinforcing its haunting, symmetrical composition and the unsettling presence of the demonic figure.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a split-screen comparison of two dark, expressive paintings, one a grotesque figure with a long white beard and a demonic companion, and the other Velázquez's 'Menippus' (1638). A narrator explains Goya's admiration for this Velázquez painting, noting Goya reproduced it as an etching, shown in a third panel labeled 'MENIPPUS', created in reverse. Menippus is identified as a satirical, cynical ancient Greek philosopher, resonating with Goya's viewpoint. The scene progresses through close-ups of etchings, including a blue-toned cherub escaping a monstrous 'menippo', leading to a split-screen displaying a cracked, monochrome surface next to a textured, dark painting. This visually represents the poor condition of works like 'Two Old Men', described as having a significant plaster crack, smudges, severe cracks, and smears. A modern conservation setup featuring a painting on a mechanical rig linked to a laptop is then presented, with the narrator commenting on the imprecision of historical restoration techniques. High-contrast X-ray or infrared images are shown, uncovering a hidden, grotesque figure beneath the surface. The name 'Salvador Martínez Cubells' appears, identified as the restorer who commenced their meticulous restoration. The segment concludes with a close-up of a dark, expressive painting featuring multiple distorted faces, which then transitions to a colorized version revealing the original brushwork and palette of the concealed figure.

Visually, it is shown in both color and monochrome X-ray views, with the X-ray revealing a hidden grotesque figure beneath. The narrator describes it as being in a 'poor state of repair with a large crack in the plaster' and having been 'smudged, badly cracked, and smeared with plaster' before restoration.

The narrator states that Goya 'admired this particular painting by Velázquez' and 'reproduced it as an etching'.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of a 1901 portrait of Salvador Martínez Cubells, an older man with a white beard and glasses, whose role in making Goya's works accessible to the public is highlighted. The scene shifts to a sepia-toned archival photograph of a chaotic event, then to a wide view of the ornate Spanish pavilion at the 1878 Exposition Universelle in Paris, featuring the painting 'Witches’ Coven.' It is revealed that Baron Émile d'Erlanger initially intended to sell Goya's paintings at this exhibition, but due to Cubells' slow progress in transferring the works, only four of fourteen were ready by 1875. This led d'Erlanger to donate the paintings to the Spanish government, coinciding with the appearance of a black-and-white archival shot of the Prado Museum and the onset of melancholic string music. The video then presents side-by-side comparisons of Goya's 'Men Reading' and 'Women Laughing' from the Black Paintings series, noting their original proximity, smaller scale, vertical orientation, lighter theme despite chromatic darkness, and identical lighting source. The segment concludes with X-ray/infrared images of these paintings, revealing underlying details and their poor state, making interpretation difficult, while the melancholic music persists. Further examination focuses on 'Women Laughing Painting,' described as unsettling with distorted grins and thick brushwork, identified as 'the key to this pair' and unusually overtly sexual for Goya, possibly created in secret. This is juxtaposed with Goya's 1799 etchings 'It is Well Pulled Up' and 'Trials.' The focus then shifts to 'Men Reading (From the Black Paintings),' depicting six men shrouded in darkness around a light source, with the central figure holding a newspaper or magazine, illuminated by a triangle of light. The speaker notes the men gather to hear the central character better, with an additional figure in the background added by a restorer. A comparison with a 1874 photograph of 'Men Reading' reveals a more intact version with a clearer face in the upper left, now obscured. The video ends by emphasizing the dark, chaotic atmosphere and expressive brushwork of 'Men Reading,' also known as 'the politicians,' with contorted faces against a nearly black background.

Prado Museum.description: A black-and-white archival shot of the neoclassical facade of the Prado Museum, which appears as the narrator explains that Baron Émile d'Erlanger ultimately donated the works to the Spanish government, with a low, melancholic string instrument beginning to play in the background.

Salvador Martínez Cubells.description: A painted portrait of an older man with a white beard, glasses, and a dark suit, identified as painted by Enrique Diosayudo in 1901, whose efforts are credited by the narrator for making the works publicly accessible.

Exposition Universelle (1878), Paris.description: A sepia-toned archival photograph of the Spanish pavilion at the 1878 Paris Exposition, featuring arched entrances and displays, where the narrator states Baron Émile d'Erlanger initially planned to sell the paintings.

X-ray/Infrared Imaging.description: High-contrast, monochrome visualizations of the 'Men Reading' and 'Women Laughing' paintings, revealing hidden underlayers, compositional changes, and textures, with visible measurement rulers along the edges, indicating technical analysis.

Men Reading (From the Black Paintings).description: A dark, expressive oil painting showing a group of six men 'shrouded in darkness,' huddled around a light source, their faces distorted and shadowed, rendered in thick, dark brushwork. The central figure holds a 'newspaper or magazine' on his lap, his shirt illuminated by a 'triangle of light' that directs the viewer's gaze. The men 'gather around the central character in order to hear him better,' with 'two more men in the background,' one gazing upwards having been 'added by the restorer.' This work has also been called 'the politicians.'

Women Laughing Painting.description: A dark, unsettling oil painting depicting two women with wide, distorted grins, their features blurred and eerie, evoking madness or mockery, shown in close-up with thick, expressive brushwork and a nearly black background. The speaker identifies it as 'the key to this pair of paintings,' describing it as 'unusual for Goya' due to its 'overtly sexual' nature, showing 'a man pleasuring himself as two hags or prostitutes laugh at him,' suggesting Goya, being 'reserved and prudish,' 'only created [it] in secret.'

Trials.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) depicting a nude woman holding a skull, with a cat, jug, and skull on the ground, rendered in stark, expressive lines.

1874 photo.description: A grainy, monochrome archival photograph showing a more intact version of the 'Men Reading' painting, with clearer facial features and less damage, particularly highlighting a circled face in the upper left that is now obscured in the modern version.

It is Well Pulled Up.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (1799) showing a woman leaning over a seated, grotesque figure, with a cat and skull nearby, rendered in fine, expressive lines.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with Goya's 'Men Reading (From the Black Paintings)', where grim-faced men are depicted, with the narrator suggesting they discuss a newspaper article about themselves and referencing historian Francisco Javier de Salas' comparison of politicians' talk to a man mocked by two women. A split-screen then shows 'Men Reading' alongside Goya's 'Women Laughing Painting', featuring two women with eerie grins, accompanied by somber, dramatic music. The focus shifts to a close-up of Goya’s 'Judith and Holofernes Painting', depicting Judith in a golden headdress with a sword and a calm expression, illustrating the Old Testament event where Judith and her maidservant behead the tyrant Holofernes, with on-screen text quoting 'Judith 13:8 Then she struck his neck twice with all her might, and cut off his head'. This is contrasted with Artemisia Gentileschi’s 1612 'Judith and Holofernes', a more violent portrayal often interpreted as a triumph over tyranny, a theme discussed in relation to Gentileschi. Returning to Goya’s version, a zoom on Judith’s face and sword emphasizes her unsettling calmness, as the narrator explains Goya’s departure from traditional portraits by removing specific settings and iconographies to focus solely on the action, noting that Goya's Judith could be a contemporary woman. A split-screen juxtaposes Goya’s 'Judith and Holofernes' with the serene 'The Milkmaid of Bordeaux', before lingering on Goya’s Judith, with the narrator concluding that the portrayal's immediacy evokes Goya's history with Ferdinand VII.

Judith and Holofernes Painting.description: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya (1827) depicting a woman in a golden headdress holding a sword, her face illuminated against a dark background, with a calm, almost serene expression, emphasizing the unsettling nature of the violent act. The narrator identifies this as the Old Testament moment when Judith and her maidservant cut off the head of the tyrant Holofernes, noting Goya's departure from traditional portraits by removing specific settings and peripheral iconographies to focus solely on the action, making Judith appear as a woman from Goya's own time, and highlighting the immediacy of the portrayal as a reminder of Goya's history with Ferdinand VII.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a close-up of Jan de Bray’s 1659 painting *Judith and Holofernes*, showcasing Judith, clad in a red dress and white headdress, poised to strike Holofernes with a sword as a maidservant watches in the dramatically lit, tense scene. The narrative then shifts, transitioning through a dark, narrow frame to Francisco Goya’s 1827 *Judith and Holofernes*, where Judith, in a golden headdress and dark dress, holds a sword with a serene expression, a stark contrast to the earlier depiction. Rhythmic metallic clanging sounds precede a move to Goya’s 1799 *Fight with Cudgels*, a dark, dynamic portrayal of two men in rough attire brutally fighting with sticks in a desolate, rocky landscape under a stormy sky, with the speaker noting such fights were still permitted in Goya's era. The camera zooms in on the men's faces, highlighting the intensity and blood on the left man's neck as the right man shields his face. A split-screen emerges, juxtaposing the original painting with a monochrome version featuring white outlines that emphasize the figures' forms and movement. The speaker questions if the men are sinking into quicksand, revealing Goya originally depicted them in high grass before substantial changes were made, placing them knee-deep in mud. The video concludes with a wide, static shot of the full *Fight with Cudgels* painting, emphasizing its chaotic energy and expressive brushwork, as the speaker interprets the scene of brutal, pointless violence as an allusion to Spain's upheaval.

Fight with Cudgels Painting.description: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya (1799) depicting two men in rough clothing engaged in a brutal fight with sticks, which the speaker notes reflects fights allowed in Goya's day where opponents would club each other to death. Accompanied by rhythmic metallic clanging sounds, the painting is shown with close-ups highlighting the intensity and blood on the man on the left's neck, while the speaker describes the men 'beating the life out of each other' and the man on the right 'shielding his face.' The speaker questions if they are sinking into quicksand, revealing that substantial changes were made to the painting after its removal, with Goya originally depicting the fighters standing in high grass rather than knee-deep in mud, ultimately interpreting the scene as an allusion to the upheaval in Spain.

event_blocks.summary: Goya's 'Fight with Cudgels Painting', a visceral depiction of conflict with visible blood and dynamic brushwork, is introduced with a voiceover interpreting it as a representation of Spain's internal strife. Somber string music accompanies a transition to a close-up of 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', a dark, expressive work portraying emaciated figures in despair, identified by on-screen text and a voiceover linking it to the theme of social disparity and gluttony, suggesting it hung above Goya's kitchen doorway. The narrative then moves to a 3D rendering of a Museum Gallery, showcasing 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting' alongside other Goya masterpieces like 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', with the voiceover noting its small size and ease of restoration. The summary concludes with a monochrome, X-ray-like image of 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', revealing its underlying structure in a translucent green hue, accompanied by the observation that X-rays indicate minimal reworking.

Museum Gallery.description: A dimly lit, spacious art gallery with polished floors and white walls, featuring large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series displayed under focused lighting, with visitors observing them in quiet contemplation, shown in a wide shot.

X-Rays.description: High-contrast, monochrome images revealing hidden layers beneath the surface of a painting, showing a grotesque, bearded figure with wide eyes and a distorted face, with visible cracks and texture. The voiceover specifically states that these 'X-rays show us it was barely reworked.'

Fight with Cudgels Painting.description: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya showing two men in rough clothing engaged in a violent struggle with sticks, set against a rocky landscape, rendered in rough, dynamic brushwork with visible blood on one man’s neck, visually representing a 'picture of tribalism, a country tearing itself apart,' which the voiceover interprets as Goya's statement, 'this is Spain.'

Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting.description: A somber, dark painting by Francisco Goya showing two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair, rendered in rough, expressive strokes. The voiceover identifies its original placement 'above the doorway to the kitchen where food was delivered to Goya by his maid,' and describes it as an 'allegory of gluttony' depicting 'the gap between the haves and the have-nots,' with 'the one on the left attacking the food with gusto,' displaying 'a manic grin on its face and purposely turning away from the other figure, who is practically a skeleton, already dead and decaying.' It is noted as 'one of the smallest of the series,' making it 'easy to remove and needed little restoration,' and later, X-rays reveal it 'was barely reworked.'

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure with horns surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, shown in full frame with the horned figure adorned with a leafy crown and surrounded by a crowd of witches and monstrous figures in a desolate landscape. The voiceover mentions this work is 'also known as the witches,' with the speaker finding the figures 'fairly genderless.'

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya, showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black, shown in the final grid montage.

Two Old Men Eating Soup'. The voiceover notes that 'This work is also known as the witches,' referring to another painting, before specifying that the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting' was likely 'above the doorway to the kitchen where food was delivered to Goya by his maid.' The speaker suggests Goya is revisiting the theme 'of the gap between the haves and the have-nots,' presented 'via an allegory of gluttony,' describing how 'the one on the left is attacking the food with gusto,' with 'a manic grin on its face and purposely turning away from the other figure, who is practically a skeleton, already dead and decaying.' A brief sketch-like drawing appears before returning to the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting'. The scene then shifts to a 3D architectural rendering of a museum gallery, where the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting' is displayed above a doorway, alongside other large, dark Goya works like the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting'. The voiceover adds that this painting is 'one of the smallest of the series,' meaning it 'was easy to remove and needed little restoration.' The final shot is a monochrome, X-ray-like image of the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', revealing its underlying structure in a ghostly, translucent green hue, with the voiceover concluding, 'X-rays show us it was barely reworked.'

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a close-up on the darkly painted, grotesque face from Francisco Goya’s *Altropos (The Fates)*, its distorted features and thick, earthy brushwork against a black background highlighted by a narrator stating, "the brushwork is exactly how Goya intended" and "the artist is at his most impressionistic here." A hand holding a spoon, rendered with similar heavy, textured strokes, is then shown, visually linking to the *Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting*, as the narrator observes the paint being "handled quickly, but with the kind of firm hand you only get with a lifetime of experience," while melancholic background music commences. The scene shifts to a full view of *Altropos (The Fates)*, depicting three emaciated figures in a desolate landscape, identified by the narrator as Goya's "interpretation of a mythological subject, the Moirai, or the sisters of fate from classical literature." This painting is juxtaposed with a classical engraving of Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos, revealing Goya’s grotesque reinterpretation of the traditional, idealized figures: Clotho, "the youngest sister," is shown holding a newborn baby instead of spinning the thread of life; Lachesis, "the second sister," holds a looking glass to inspect the thread; and Altropos, "the eldest sister," carries scissors to cut the thread of life. Close-ups contrast Goya’s dark, monstrous forms with the engraving’s smooth, classical anatomy, with a white outline isolating the central figure, Lachesis, emphasizing her distorted, skeletal form. The narrator concludes by pointing out Goya's addition of a "fourth figure, not featured in the myth, a man with his hands tied behind his back," suggesting this might represent Goya himself, "helpless to his fate, controlled by outside forces," a notion underscored by the stark contrast between Goya’s dark vision of decay, fate, and psychological horror and the Enlightenment-era idealism of the engraving.

Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting.description: A somber, dark painting by Francisco Goya showing two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair, rendered in rough, expressive strokes, shown in a close-up of a hand holding a spoon, linking it to the *Altropos* painting.

Altropos (The Fates) Painting.description: A dark, haunting oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819–1823) depicting three grotesque, dark figures in a desolate landscape, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork, evoking themes of fate and decay. The narrator identifies it as Goya's 'interpretation of a mythological subject, the Moirai, or the sisters of fate from classical literature,' detailing the roles of Clotho (who Goya depicts holding a newborn baby instead of spinning thread), Lachesis (holding a looking glass to inspect the thread), and Atropos (carrying scissors to cut the thread of life). The narrator also points out a 'fourth figure, not featured in the myth, a man with his hands tied behind his back,' which he speculates could be Goya himself, 'helpless to his fate, controlled by outside forces.'

Classical Engraving of the Fates.description: A detailed, classical engraving depicting the three Fates—Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos—as idealized, nude or semi-nude figures in flowing drapery, seated on rocks, with labels beneath each figure, rendered in soft, naturalistic tones. It is used for direct comparison with Goya’s dark reinterpretation, representing the traditional depiction of the Fates whose roles (spinning, inspecting, cutting the thread of life) are described by the narrator.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with a detailed examination of Francisco Goya's 'Altropos (The Fates) Painting,' a dark and chaotic work featuring emaciated figures and a desolate landscape rendered in thick, expressive brushwork of browns and blacks, with a male voice explaining the personal significance of a figure holding a child, relating it to Goya's family tragedies of numerous miscarriages and infant deaths, with only one surviving child. The narration delves into classical mythology, describing the Fates' role in determining a child's life after birth, contrasting Clotho's role at birth with Altropos's decree of fate, and noting a fourth figure, possibly Goya, unable to intervene. As somber string music begins, the visual sequence rapidly shifts from 'Altropos' to Goya's bright, pastoral 'Little Giants' (1792) depicting playful children, then to the same painting with the children removed to symbolize loss of innocence, followed by William Blake’s dark, mystical 'The Night of Enitharmon’s Joy' (1795), and finally returning to a full view of 'Altropos' revealing four monstrous figures, one with a magnifying glass, another with a raised hand, and a distant child. The scene then transitions to 'La Leocadia,' a somber painting of a woman in a black dress, identified as Leocadia Weiss, Goya's maid and probable lover, leaning over a stone ledge with her face illuminated against a pale, cloudy sky; the narrator highlights Goya's departure from a black base color in this work. The focus shifts to 'La Leocadia,' depicting Leocadia Weiss, Goya's maid and probable lover, her face illuminated against a pale, cloudy sky as she leans over a stone ledge in a black dress, with the narrator noting this title was not Goya's own. A split-screen comparison is presented with another portrait identified as 'Leocadia' by Goya from 1815, believed by many historians to be of the same woman, to which the black painting bears a strong resemblance. A black-and-white profile sketch of Josefa Bayeu, Goya’s wife, from 1805, is shown, providing context for the narrator's mention of Goya's wife's death. This is followed by a sepia-toned historical photograph of 'Quinta del Sordo (House of the Deaf),' Goya’s rural retreat where Leocadia stayed with him until 1824. The scene then moves to a modern-day exterior of Goya’s last apartment in Bordeaux, marked by a plaque and a bronze bust, signifying his move to France in 1824, accompanied by Leocadia and her daughter Rosario, who remained with him until his death in 1828. A dimly lit interior scene from the 1999 film 'Goya in Bordeaux' shows people gathered around a piano. The concluding frames display two black-and-white etchings: a self-portrait of Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla from the 1830s, identified as Leocadia's daughter Rosario whom Goya taught to paint and who became a painter herself, with many believing her to be Goya's illegitimate child; and a profile portrait of Goya from 1826, also by Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla, capturing his aged, contemplative face.

Altropos (The Fates) Painting.description: A dark, haunting oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819–1823), identified by a male voice as depicting 'Altropos' and relating to 'Clotho,' features grotesque, dark figures in a desolate landscape, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork. The voice explains the painting's personal meaning for Goya, whose family life was plagued by miscarriages and infant deaths, noting that the figure holding a baby replaces 'Clotho's spinning tool.' It further elaborates on classical mythology where the sisters of fate determine a child's life, with 'Clotho' presiding over birth and the baby still attached by the 'thread of life,' but by 'Altropos,' the thread is cut, deciding the baby's cruel fate. The painting, shown in full frame, reveals four monstrous figures, one holding a magnifying glass, another with a raised hand, and a small child figure in the distance, with the voice suggesting the fourth figure, possibly Goya, is unable to help.

La Leocadia Painting.description: A somber, dark painting showing a woman in a long black dress leaning over a stone ledge, her face illuminated against a pale, cloudy sky, with a metal railing visible in the background, evoking melancholy and introspection. The narrator identifies the woman as Leocadia Weiss, Goya's maid and probable lover, also known as 'the Seductress,' noting that the current title was not Goya's own.

The Night of Enitharmon’s Joy Painting.description: A mystical, dark painting by William Blake (1795) depicting nude figures in a rocky, otherworldly landscape, with a central woman with long black hair and a serene expression, rendered in earthy tones with a dark, starry background.

Little Giants Painting.description: A bright, pastoral oil painting by Francisco Goya (1792) showing three children playing, one riding on another’s shoulders, with the child on top wearing a red jacket and wide-brimmed hat, set against a pale sky and green landscape; the painting is shown first in full, then with the children removed, leaving only the adult figure.

Goya’s last apartment in Bordeaux.description: A modern-day exterior shot of a stone building with a plaque reading 'COURS DE L’INTENDANCE' and a bronze bust of Goya, indicating the location of his final residence in Bordeaux. The narrator explains that Goya abandoned Spain for France in 1824, and Leocadia followed him with her daughter Rosario, staying there until his death in 1828.

Self-Portrait (Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla, 1.description: A black-and-white etching of a woman with a wide-brimmed hat and delicate features, rendered in fine, expressive lines, with a crown-like emblem in the upper left corner. The narrator identifies her as Leocadia's daughter, Rosario, whom Goya taught how to paint, and who later had a career as a painter in France and Spain. Many, including the narrator, believe she is Goya's illegitimate child.

Goya in Bordeaux (Film Clip).description: A dimly lit interior scene from the 1999 film 'Goya in Bordeaux' by Carlos Saura, showing a group of people gathered around a piano in a room with a chandelier, evoking a somber, intimate atmosphere.

Portrait of Goya (Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla.description: A black-and-white profile etching of an aged Goya, showing his contemplative face with deep lines, receding hair, and a high collar, rendered in fine, expressive lines. This portrait was created by Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla, Leocadia's daughter, whom Goya taught to paint.

Josefa Bayeu (Goya’s wife) Portrait.description: A black-and-white profile sketch from 1805, rendered in fine, textured lines, showing Goya’s wife with her hair styled in an updo, her face in profile with a serene expression. The narrator mentions her death as the event that led to Leocadia moving in with Goya.

Quinta del Sordo (House of the Deaf).description: A sepia-toned historical photograph showing a stone building with ivy-covered walls and an arched doorway, with three figures in 19th-century attire standing before it, identified as Goya’s rural retreat. The narrator explains that Leocadia stayed with Goya in this countryside location until 1824.

(Identified as) Leocadia Painting.description: A formal portrait of a woman with curly hair in a lighter, more formal dress, painted by Francisco Goya in 1815, shown in split-screen comparison with 'La Leocadia.' The narrator states that most historians believe this well-known portrait is of Leocadia, and the 'black painting' (La Leocadia) bears a strong resemblance to it.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with Francisco Goya’s *The Milkmaid of Bordeaux*, an impressionistic 1827 portrait depicting Leocadia, a woman described as striking and fiery, whose demeanor often displeased Goya, followed by an account of her later poverty, living in rented rooms and selling Goya drawings for sustenance. The visual then shifts to *La Leocadia*, a somber 1827 painting featuring a woman in a dark dress, identified by the narrator as a 'funereal dress' typical of Madrid's lower-class women, known as 'Maja,' although Leocadia herself would not have worn such attire. This transitions to *Majas on a Balcony* (1812), a detailed painting of two women in ornate golden dresses, accompanied by an audio description of Goya's fascination with the Majas' elaborate outfits, style, and playful behavior. The sequence then moves to *The White Duchess* (1795), a full-length portrait of the Duchess of Alba, a prominent Spanish noble, adorned in elegant French fashion, with the narrator disclosing Goya's infatuation with her. The event concludes with a split-screen comparison featuring *The Black Duchess* (1797), portraying the same woman in a black dress two years later, a depiction the audio suggests was 'scandalous'.

The Milkmaid of Bordeaux Painting.description: A soft, impressionistic 1827 portrait of a woman in profile, with dark hair and a light headscarf, her face gently illuminated against a muted greenish background, rendered in loose, expressive brushwork. The subject is believed to be Leocadia, described by the narrator as a striking, good-looking woman with a strong, fiery character whose manner often upset Goya. She was left out of his will, lived in poverty, and sold his drawings to survive.

The White Duchess Painting.description: A full-length 1795 portrait of the Duchess of Alba, one of Spain's leading nobles, in a white dress with a red sash and bow, standing in a landscape with a small dog at her feet, her dark hair adorned with a red flower, rendered in bright, formal lighting. The narrator identifies her as the Duchess of Alba, dressed in elegant and expensive French fashions of the day, and reveals Goya's infatuation with her.

The Black Duchess Painting.description: A full-length 1797 portrait of the Duchess of Alba, the same woman as in *The White Duchess*, now in a black dress with gold trim, maintaining the same pose and background, creating a stark contrast in color and mood. The narrator notes that Goya painted her in this portrait two years later, hinting at a 'scandalous' depiction.

La Leocadia Painting.description: A somber, dark 1827 painting of a woman in a long black dress leaning over a stone ledge, her face illuminated against a pale, cloudy sky, with a metal railing visible in the background, evoking melancholy and introspection. Goya portrays her in a dark, almost funereal dress, characteristic of lower-class women of Madrid known as 'Maja,' though the narrator explains that in reality, Leocadia would not have worn such clothes as they were beneath her.

Majas on a Balcony Painting.description: A richly detailed 1812 painting of two women in ornate golden dresses with lace veils, leaning on a black railing, their expressions serene and composed, set against a dark background. The narrator explains that Goya was fixated on the 'Maja's' elaborate outfits, sense of style in dress, and cheeky behavior, as depicted here.

I am Still Learning Painting.description: A sketchy, monochromatic 1826 drawing of a woman in a white dress kneeling before an old man, rendered in loose charcoal strokes with minimal detail and a dark, undefined background.

A Woman Kneeling Before an Old Man Paint.description: A sketchy, monochromatic 1820 drawing showing a woman in a white dress kneeling before an old man, with more defined figures than the previous sketch, rendered in dark, expressive lines.

event_blocks.summary: The segment begins with a close-up of the somber *La Leocadia Painting*, showing a melancholic woman in a black shawl and veil against a cloudy sky; a speaker describes her attire as a 'low cut, maha outfit,' an 'erotic proletariat type' that appealed to Goya, leaning against what is speculated to be Goya's grave, mourning his end, with experts interpreting her as the personification of melancholy meditating on life and death. The scene transitions to a high-contrast X-ray view of the painting, revealing hidden layers and compositional changes, such as her originally leaning on a door or fireplace without a veil, which the speaker notes signifies mourning, suggesting Goya later added a darker, more despondent theme, concluding that *La Leocadia* is one of Goya's final works in the series. Somber string music plays as the video cuts to *21. Fantastic Vision*, a dynamic painting of two figures in motion, identified by the speaker as having been named by Goya's friend, Antonio Brugad. This is followed by a black-and-white engraving of *Asmodeus (Dictionnaire Infernal, 1818)*, which the speaker explains represents the prince of demons and hell in Jewish legend, before returning to the *Fantastic Vision* painting, focusing on the central figures.

event_blocks.summary: Archival Footage introduces the dynamic, surreal painting 'Fantastic Vision,' depicting two terrified floating figures, one female covering her face as if fleeing, and one male pointing towards a distant rocky landscape with a cliff-top settlement, possibly the Rock of Gibraltar. A chaotic crowd of tiny figures on horseback and foot below are identified as refugees pursued by the French army from the 3rd of May 1808, aiming at the defenseless. A somber, melancholic plucked string instrument begins as the scene shifts to a split-screen comparison of two dark, violent paintings featuring figures with rifles in thick brushwork. This is followed by a cut to the Saturn Devouring His Son Painting, a dark, visceral depiction of a gaunt, wild-eyed figure devouring a child in stark red and black contrasts, described as 'the most famous of the black paintings,' 'the most horrific and the most disgust,' and likely shocking to 19th-century Spanish audiences. The video then transitions to The Nightmare, showing a woman on a bed with a demonic figure on her chest, rendered in dramatic chiaroscuro. The final frames show a black-and-white, grainy archival clip of a man resembling a vampire leaning over a bed, with On-Screen Text indicating 'Frankenstein' was published in 1818 and 'The Vampyre' in 1819, contextualizing these visuals within 'the rise of Romanticism and its obsession with the gory and the supernatural.'

Archival Footage.description: Grainy black-and-white footage depicting a man with a bald head and pointed ears, resembling a vampire, leaning over a bed, which the narrator links to the rise of Romanticism and its obsession with the gory and the supernatural.

The Nightmare.description: A dark, dramatic painting by Henry Fuseli (1781) depicting a woman lying on a bed with a demonic figure perched on her chest, rendered in dramatic chiaroscuro with a dark, shadowy atmosphere, which the narrator links to the rise of Romanticism and its obsession with the gory and the supernatural.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya, showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black. The narrator identifies it as 'the most famous of the black paintings,' describing it as 'the most horrific and the most disgust,' and suggesting it was likely more shocking to modern viewers than to 19th-century Spanish audiences.

The female covers her face and looks away as if being chased, while the male points to a town at the top of a mountain, identified by the narrator as possibly the Rock of Gibraltar, a refuge for Spanish liberals during the Peninsular War. The crowd below, with horses and wagons, are described by the narrator as refugees fleeing danger, with the French army from the 3rd of May 1808 in the foreground, taking aim at them.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a black-and-white engraving of a chaotic, theatrical scene, possibly a witches’ sabbath, featuring a central demonic figure with horns surrounded by grotesque figures under a dark, draped ceiling, while a narrator introduces the "Gothic novel" and "Phantasmagoria," an entertainment involving projected images of monsters. The scene shifts to an abstract, rapidly moving image of a winged, monstrous figure, followed by a close-up of Francisco Goya's *Saturn Devouring His Son Painting*, which the narrator describes as "particularly grotesque" and "even more horrifying that Goya painted it on the walls of his dining room." A dimly lit corridor reveals two framed paintings of violent mythological scenes, prompting the narrator to explain the myth of Cronos, also known as Saturn, who "overthrew his father" and, "afraid that history would repeat itself, he ate, or rather swallowed whole, five out of his six children." This narrative is visually supported by Giovanni Romanelli's *Chronos and his Child* (1650s), Giorgio Vasari/Cristoforo Gherardi's *The Mutilation of Uranus by Saturn* (16th century), and an ancient Greek vase (460–450 BCE) depicting Cronos handing a baby to a woman. Returning to Goya's *Saturn Devouring His Son Painting*, the narrator highlights its distinctiveness compared to previous artistic interpretations. Peter Paul Rubens' *Saturn* (1636) is then presented, showing a muscular Saturn holding a child. The narrator concludes by emphasizing that Goya's Saturn "is not swallowing the body whole, but gnawing down on it in a frenzied and horrific attack," and that "it is not a baby's body, as in the myth, but a grown adult's, which makes it even more disturbing," as the video zooms in on the consumed body in Goya's work, accentuating the visceral horror with red and white paint strokes.

The Mutilation of Uranus by Saturn Paint.description: A 16th-century fresco by Giorgio Vasari/Cristoforo Gherardi depicting a violent mythological scene with a large celestial globe, a figure holding a scythe, and other figures in torment. This painting illustrates the mythological tale of the Greek god Cronos (Roman Saturn) who overthrew his father.

Witches’ Sabbath Painting.description: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a demonic figure with horns surrounded by grotesque, writhing figures in a nightmarish scene; characterized by thick, expressive brushwork and a deeply unsettling atmosphere, shown in full frame with the horned figure adorned with a leafy crown and surrounded by a crowd of witches and monstrous figures in a desolate landscape.

Saturn Painting (Rubens).description: A 1636 oil painting by Peter Paul Rubens depicting a muscular, bearded Saturn holding a child in his arms, about to devour it, with a star above, rendered in dramatic lighting and rich colors. This represents an earlier artistic interpretation of the mythological tale of Cronos (Roman Saturn) who ate his children, a version the narrator contrasts with Goya's later, more disturbing depiction.

Chronos and his Child Painting.description: A Baroque painting by Giovanni Romanelli (1650s) depicting a winged, bearded Chronos holding a scythe and a child, rendered in dramatic chiaroscuro with a cloudy sky background. This illustrates the mythological tale of the Greek god Cronos, whom the Romans called Saturn, who overthrew his father and, fearing a similar fate, ate five out of his six children.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya, showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a grown adult, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black. The narrator highlights its particularly grotesque 'cannibalism angle' and the horrifying fact that Goya painted it on the walls of his dining room, depicting Saturn gnawing down on the body in a frenzied and horrific attack, making it even more disturbing than the mythological account as it's not a baby's body but a grown adult's.

Cronos Handed a Baby Vase.description: An ancient Greek red-figure vase (460–450 BCE) showing a bearded Cronos handing a baby to a woman, rendered in stylized, orange figures on a black background. This depicts an aspect of the mythological tale of Cronos (Roman Saturn) who, fearing his children would overthrow him, ate them, though in some versions, a stone was substituted for a child.

raw_segments: The video opens with a detailed black-and-white engraving of a chaotic, theatrical scene, possibly a witches’ sabbath, featuring a central demonic figure with horns, surrounded by writhing, grotesque figures under a dark, draped ceiling. A male narrator sets the context, mentioning the "Gothic novel" and "Phantasmagoria," a popular entertainment in Madrid where "images of monsters and ghoulish subject matter were projected in theaters using lanterns." The scene transitions to a dark, abstract, and rapidly moving image of a winged, monstrous figure in distorted, colorful brushstrokes, followed by a close-up of Francisco Goya's *Saturn Devouring His Son Painting*. The narrator describes the "cannibalism angle" as "particularly grotesque" and "even more horrifying that Goya painted it on the walls of his dining room." The visual then shifts to a dimly lit corridor with two framed paintings depicting violent mythological scenes. The narrator explains that "the story is from the mythological tale of the Greek god Cronos," whom "the Romans called him Saturn," who "overthrew his father" and, "afraid that history would repeat itself, he ate, or rather swallowed whole, five out of his six children." This mythological context is then illustrated by a series of classical paintings: Giovanni Romanelli's *Chronos and his Child* (1650s), Giorgio Vasari/Cristoforo Gherardi's *The Mutilation of Uranus by Saturn* (16th century), and an ancient Greek vase (460–450 BCE) showing Cronos handing a baby to a woman. The video returns to Goya's *Saturn Devouring His Son Painting*, focusing on the monstrous face, as the narrator notes that while "it has been tackled before by artists," "Goya's version is different." Peter Paul Rubens' *Saturn* (1636) is then shown, depicting a muscular, bearded Saturn holding a child about to devour it. The narrator concludes by emphasizing that in Goya's painting, "Saturn is not swallowing the body whole, but gnawing down on it in a frenzied and horrific attack," and that "it is not a baby's body, as in the myth, but a grown adult's, which makes it even more disturbing," as the final frames zoom in on the child’s body being consumed in Goya's work, emphasizing the visceral horror with red and white paint strokes.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with Goya's 'Time Devouring Men,' a red chalk drawing of a skeletal figure consuming a human, followed by the visceral oil painting 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' where the gaunt figure's terrifying expression reveals shock at his own monstrous act. The narrator emphasizes Saturn's awareness and inability to stop, focusing on the wide eyes and bloody limb. This theme of horror continues with a chaotic painting of a violent struggle and the etchings 'This is What You Were Born For,' depicting a man stabbing a corpse amidst a pile, and 'This is Worse,' showing a man in despair. Goya transcribed the 'hell on earth' he witnessed in Madrid—innocent citizens cut down, starving bodies, and rotting corpses—for future generations, with 'Saturn Devouring His Son' ultimately reflecting on power and its extremes. The segment then shifts to 'The Execution of José María Torrijos Painting,' a grim scene of blindfolded men facing a firing squad, linking Ferdinand VII's actions to Saturn's, as those in power will use any means to retain it, even consuming their own. A grotesque, wide-eyed face from another painting emphasizes horror, accompanied by eerie string music. The narrator then posits that 'The Drowning Dog Painting,' showing a small dog's head barely above murky water, is the most pessimistic image, a dog's head gazing upwards, lost and drowning, looking beyond the frame. An X-ray reveals faint marks above the dog, and a split-screen comparison shows that two birds, initially in the sky, disappeared when the painting was transferred to canvas. The atmosphere is somber, exploring death and hidden meaning, underscored by melancholic music. The scene transitions to Hieronymus Bosch’s *The Last Judgment*, a chaotic surreal depiction, before showing a desolate landscape with a small, dark dog under a pale sky, which zooms in and becomes ethereal. The narrator states this dog, Goya's stand-in for humanity, struggles alone, looking for a miracle that will never come. Melancholy classical guitar music begins as a sepia-toned aerial view of Bordeaux appears, with the text 'The Truth.' Goya, a patriot in exile, died in Bordeaux in 1828. This leads to Goya’s etching *Truth Has Died*, depicting a lifeless nude woman surrounded by a distressed crowd, implicitly linked to his 'black paintings' where sacrifice offers no salvation. A bronze statue of Goya in Bordeaux is shown, followed by an archival photograph of men around a monument, as the narrator introduces a 'macabre twist' to Goya's story in 1901. A close-up of Pieter Claesz’s *Vanitas Still Life*, featuring a skull and hourglass, symbolizes mortality. Grainy archival footage from the Spanish newspaper *ABC* shows men gathered around a monument during Goya's body's exhumation for transfer to Spain, during which his skull mysteriously went missing, leading the narrator to remark that the painter, skilled at understanding others, lost his own head. A portrait of Charles III in Hunting Dress and Goya’s *Self-Portrait at an Easel* are shown, with Goya identified as 'the most radical artist,' 'the last of the old masters and the first of the moderns,' defined by 'truth.' Goya's *Two Old Men Painting*, *The Third of May 1808* depicting a man before a firing squad, and modern news footage of a fiery explosion with a 'CIVILIANS UNDER ATTACK' chyron are presented. The narrator describes Goya as a 'prescient artist' and 'chronicler of a violent age' who presented 'uncomfortable truths.' The segment concludes with a close-up of *Saturn Devouring His Son*, as the narrator reflects that Goya showed ordinary people are capable of evil and extraordinary things, suggesting that sometimes, when looking for monsters, we should look within ourselves, as indicated by the initial black screen and text 'By James Payne,' accompanied by a sustained, ominous hum.

This is What You Were Born For Etching.description: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a man standing over a pile of dead bodies, one of which he is stabbing with a knife, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic contrast. This image, along with others, is thematically linked by the narrator to the 'hell on earth' Goya witnessed in Madrid, including innocent citizens cut down and piles of corpses.

This is Worse Etching.description: An etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man in despair clutching his head, with another figure behind him holding a knife, rendered in expressive, dark lines. This image, along with others, is thematically linked by the narrator to the 'hell on earth' Goya witnessed in Madrid, including poverty, hunger, and piles of corpses.

Time Devouring Men Painting.description: A red chalk drawing by Francisco Goya (1797) showing a monstrous, skeletal figure with long hair devouring a human form, rendered in expressive, sketchy lines with a focus on the act of consumption, which the narrator confirms was intended to depict an adult.

Saturn Devouring His Son Painting.description: A dark, visceral oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819–1823) showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black, shown in close-up focusing on the face and the act of consumption, which the narrator uses to emphasize Goya's message that 'ordinary people are capable of evil' and that sometimes, 'when we are looking for monsters,' we should look closer.

The Execution of José María Torrijos Pai.description: A historical oil painting depicting a grim execution scene on a beach with blindfolded men, soldiers, and bodies, accompanied by on-screen text about the 1831 execution ordered by Ferdinand VII. The accompanying male voice draws a parallel between Ferdinand and Saturn, discussing the abuse of "pain power" and mentioning the "adult corpse" in the context of those in power mistreating citizens.

The Drowning Dog Painting.description: A dark, minimalist oil painting titled '23. The Drowning Dog,' showing only the head of a small dog barely above murky water against a desolate background. The male voice describes it as "the most pessimistic and most wretched image of them all," depicting the dog "gazing upwards, lost in the vastness... alone and drowning slowly in quicksand," looking "at someone or something outside of the composition." The audio further reveals that X-rays show the dog was originally looking at two birds flying in the sky, which disappeared when the painting was transferred from walls to canvas.

X-Rays.description: High-contrast, monochrome images revealing faint, hidden marks above the dog’s head in 'The Drowning Dog Painting,' shown in a split-screen comparison with the original. The accompanying male voice explains these X-rays reveal that the dog was originally looking at two birds flying in the sky, which were later removed or obscured when the painting was transferred from its original wall to canvas.

Archival Footage.description: Grainy black-and-white footage from the front page of *ABC* newspaper (November 30, 1919) showing men in early 20th-century attire gathered around a stone monument, handling a wooden structure, suggesting a historical ceremony, which the narrator contextualizes by explaining that Goya's body was exhumed for transfer back to Spain, but his skull was mysteriously missing, a fact that remains a mystery.

The Last Judgment.description: A dark, surreal oil painting by Hieronymus Bosch (1486) showing a chaotic scene with a fish-headed creature, a bowl with nude figures, a monkey fishing from a cage, and other grotesque elements, rendered in earthy tones and intricate detail, setting a tone of horrific imagery as described by the narrator.

Goya statue in Bordeaux.description: A modern bronze statue of Francisco Goya standing before a classical building with barred windows, wearing a long coat and holding a hat, positioned on a pedestal with a black iron fence in front, representing the artist in the city where he died in exile.

Bordeaux.description: A sepia-toned aerial view of the city of Bordeaux, showing rooftops, buildings, and a prominent church spire, with the text 'Bordeaux' overlaid. The narrator explains that Francisco Goya, 'a patriot in exile,' died here in 1828 after leaving Spain at the age of 78.

Truth Has Died.description: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a nude, lifeless woman lying on the ground, surrounded by a crowd of distressed figures, rendered in dense cross-hatching and expressive lines. The narrator implicitly links this work to Goya's 'black paintings,' where there is 'no sense that the sacrifice of human life is going to lead to salvation.'

Two Old Men Painting.description: A dark, expressive oil painting showing two grotesque, emaciated figures in a close embrace, one with a long white beard and dark robes, the other a demonic-looking figure with a mouth agape in a silent scream, which the narrator implicitly links to Goya's role as a 'chronicler of a violent age' who presented 'uncomfortable truths' about the capacity for evil.

The Third of May 1808.description: A powerful oil painting by Francisco Goya (1814) depicting a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance, serving as a prime example of Goya's 'uncomfortable truths' about violence and the capacity for evil in 'ordinary people', as highlighted by the narrator.

Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting.description: A portrait of Francisco Goya (1790) in a red dress and black hat, holding a paintbrush and palette, standing before an easel, representing his earlier, confident artistic phase.

Vanitas Still Life.description: A somber oil painting by Pieter Claesz (1630) featuring a human skull on books, a glass, a pipe, and an hourglass, rendered in muted browns and grays under chiaroscuro lighting, symbolizing mortality.

News Broadcast Footage.description: Modern, shaky video footage with a red and blue chyron reading 'DEVELOPING NOW: CIVILIANS UNDER ATTACK', showing a fiery explosion and people fleeing through rubble, creating a jarring contemporary parallel to Goya's historical depictions of violence and 'uncomfortable truths' about human nature, as highlighted by the narrator.

Charles III in Hunting Dress Painting.description: A formal portrait of Charles III (1786) in a gray hunting coat, white cravat, and tricorn hat, holding a rifle, set against a neutral background, reflecting his regal yet active persona.

By James Payne.description: White serif text centered on a black background, appearing and disappearing in a fade-in/fade-out sequence, serving as a credit or author attribution.

global_entity_library.A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting: A crowded, frenzied scene by Francisco Goya showing a religious procession with figures in ecstatic or frenzied states, their faces contorted, rendered in dark, expressive brushwork; the video focuses on a group of women in dark shawls and head coverings, one holding a small object, with a white outline highlighting a specific woman in the foreground.
global_entity_library.Women Laughing Painting: A dark, unsettling oil painting depicting two women with wide, distorted grins, their features blurred and eerie, evoking madness or mockery, shown in close-up with thick, expressive brushwork and a nearly black background, which the narrator associates with the solitary pleasure of a man being mocked.
global_entity_library.Altropos (The Fates) Painting: A dark, haunting oil painting by Francisco Goya (1819–1823), identified by a male voice as depicting 'Altropos' and relating to 'Clotho,' features grotesque, dark figures in a desolate landscape, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork. The voice explains the painting's personal meaning for Goya, whose family life was plagued by miscarriages and infant deaths, noting that the figure holding a baby replaces 'Clotho's spinning tool.' It further elaborates on classical mythology where the sisters of fate determine a child's life, with 'Clotho' presiding over birth and the baby still attached by the 'thread of life,' but by 'Altropos,' the thread is cut, deciding the baby's cruel fate. The painting, shown in full frame, reveals four monstrous figures, one holding a magnifying glass, another with a raised hand, and a small child figure in the distance, with the voice suggesting the fourth figure, possibly Goya, is unable to help.
global_entity_library.Boys Playing at Being Soldiers Painting: A cheerful, pastoral scene by Francisco Goya (1777) showing three children dressed as soldiers, one holding a rifle, another a drum, and the third a flag, set under leafy trees, reflecting a playful, light-hearted tone.
global_entity_library.Majo With Guitar (Detail) Painting: A close-up detail of a man in a tricorn hat with a contemplative, upward gaze, from Francisco Goya’s 1779 painting, showcasing his early, more naturalistic and expressive style, shown in the final grid montage, and mentioned by the narrator as one of '14 of them in total'.
global_entity_library.Charles IV in Red Painting: A formal, richly colored portrait of Charles IV (1789) in a red robe with a blue sash and medals, set against a dark, dramatic background, emphasizing his royal authority and Goya’s mastery of portraiture, as he was one of the "three Spanish kings" Goya worked for.
global_entity_library.Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting: A portrait of Francisco Goya (1790) in a red dress and black hat, holding a paintbrush and palette, standing before an easel, representing his earlier, confident artistic phase.
global_entity_library.Aerial View of Madrid Bullring: An aerial shot of a large, circular, historic bullring in Madrid, surrounded by urban buildings, with the year 1775 superimposed, providing historical and geographical context for Goya’s early life and work, as the narrator states Goya arrived in Madrid in "1775" and began working on "tapestry cartoons for the royal residences," leading to his "first contact with the Spanish Royal Family."
global_entity_library.Charles IV of Spain and his Family (Deta: A formal portrait by Francisco Goya (1800) of the Spanish royal family, with Goya’s self-portrait subtly visible in the background, observing the scene, highlighting his role as court painter after being appointed in 1789 and working for "three Spanish kings."

global_entity_library.Two Old Men Painting: A dark, expressive oil painting showing two grotesque, emaciated figures in a close embrace, one with a long white beard and dark robes, the other a demonic-looking figure with a mouth agape in a silent scream, which the narrator implicitly links to Goya's role as a 'chronicler of a violent age' who presented 'uncomfortable truths' about the capacity for evil.

global_entity_library.Self-Portrait Painting: A painted self-portrait of Francisco Goya (1815) with dark, curly hair, a weary expression, and a dark coat, set against a dark background, reflecting his later, introspective phase.
global_entity_library.The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters Et: A black-and-white etching of a sleeping man surrounded by owls and bats, symbolizing the dangers of ignorance, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' visible on a block beside him, rendered in dense cross-hatching and dramatic contrast.
global_entity_library.Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painti: A somber self-portrait of Goya appearing frail and ill, supported by Doctor Arrieta who holds a glass, painted in 1820, shown in a close-up with dark, expressive brushwork and a dark background, which the narrator uses to discuss Goya's deafness and refute claims of madness, stating Goya 'had been deaf for over 30 years at this point, and there is no evidence of madness in his letters or elsewhere'.
global_entity_library.The Second of May 1808 Painting: A chaotic battle scene by Francisco Goya (1814) depicting Spanish civilians and French soldiers in violent struggle, with horses rearing and figures in motion, rendered in earthy tones and dynamic composition, audibly illustrating the 'spontaneous rioting' on 'May the 2nd, 1808' where 'Spanish citizens rose up against the French occupiers,' accompanied by a gunshot and horse whinny.
global_entity_library.The Marquise de la Solana Painting: A delicate portrait of a woman in a white shawl with a pink flower in her hair, painted in 1793.
global_entity_library.A Way of Flying Etching: A grotesque black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, depicting a winged, demonic figure with a long tongue flying over a landscape, accompanied by smaller flying creatures, rendered in stark, expressive lines.
global_entity_library.And They are Like Wild Beasts Etching: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, portraying frenzied, animalistic figures in combat, emphasizing brutality and despair, accompanied by somber classical string music.
global_entity_library.Coronation of Napoleon Painting: A grand historical painting by Jacques Louis-David (1804) showing Napoleon placing a crown on his own head, surrounded by courtiers and clergy, with a woman in white in the background, rendered in rich, detailed brushwork, depicting Napoleon Bonaparte's self-coronation.
